title.zh: 卷三十九 劉趙淳于江劉周趙列傳
title.en: Volume 39: Biographies of Liu, Zhao, Chunyu, Jiang, Liu, Zhou, Zhao
title.raw: 卷三十九 劉趙淳于江劉周趙列傳 Volume 39: Biographies of Liu, Zhao, Chunyu, Jiang, Liu, Zhou, Zhao

sentences.zh: 後漢書卷三十九 劉趙淳於江劉周趙列傳 第二十九

translations.literal: Book of Later Han, Volume 39: Biographies of Liu, Zhao, Chunyu, Jiang, Liu, Zhou, Zhao, Number 29.
translations.idiomatic: Book of Later Han, volume 39—the collective biographies of Liu, Zhao, Chunyu, Jiang, Liu, Zhou, and Zhao—chapter twenty-nine.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「夫孝莫大於嚴父，嚴父莫大於配天，則周公其人也。

translations.literal: Confucius said: "Of filial piety none is greater than honoring the father; of honoring the father none is greater than matching Heaven—and that person was the Duke of Zhou."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, "Nothing ranks higher in filial duty than honoring one's father, and nothing honors the father more than pairing him with Heaven in sacrifice—the Duke of Zhou did exactly that."

sentences.zh: 」[一]子路曰：

translations.literal: " [Note 1] Zilu said:
translations.idiomatic: " [Note 1] Zilu said:

sentences.zh: 「傷哉貧也！

translations.literal: "How wretched is poverty!"
translations.idiomatic: "How bitter is poverty!"

sentences.zh: 生無以養，死無以葬。

translations.literal: In life there is no means to support; in death there is no means to bury."
translations.idiomatic: Alive, one cannot care for parents; dead, one cannot bury them."

sentences.zh: 」子曰：「啜菽飲水，孝也。

translations.literal: " The Master said: "Eating beans and drinking water—that is filial piety."
translations.idiomatic: " Confucius replied, "Grinding beans and drinking plain water can still be true filial devotion."

sentences.zh: 」[二]夫鐘鼓非樂雲之本，而器不可去；

translations.literal: " [Note 2] Now bells and drums are not the root of music's saying, yet the instruments cannot be removed;
translations.idiomatic: " [Note 2] Bells and drums are not what music is ultimately about, yet you cannot do without the instruments;

sentences.zh: [三]三牲非致孝之主，而養不可廢。

translations.literal: [Note 3] The three sacrificial beasts are not the master of achieving filial piety, yet providing for parents cannot be abandoned.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 3] The three sacrificial animals are not the heart of true devotion, yet supporting one's parents must never cease.

sentences.zh: [四]存器而忘本，樂之遁也；

translations.literal: [Note 4] To keep the instruments and forget the root is music's loss;
translations.idiomatic: [Note 4] Clinging to instruments while forgetting the purpose is to lose the meaning of music;

sentences.zh: [五]調器以和聲，樂之成也。

translations.literal: [Note 5] To tune instruments to harmonize tones is music's completion.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 5] tuning instruments to blend the tones—that completes music.

sentences.zh: 崇養以傷行，孝之累也；

translations.literal: To exalt provision so as to harm conduct is filial piety's burden;
translations.idiomatic: Lavish support that corrupts one's conduct burdens filial duty.

sentences.zh: [六]修己以致祿，養之大也。

translations.literal: [Note 6] To cultivate oneself so as to obtain salary is the greatness of providing.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 6] Cultivating oneself to earn an honorable stipend—that is the highest form of care.

sentences.zh: 故言能大養，則周公之祀，致四海之祭；

translations.literal: Therefore speaking of being able greatly to provide, then the Duke of Zhou's sacrifices brought the offerings of the four seas;
translations.idiomatic: Thus one who can provide on the grand scale, like the Duke of Zhou, draws sacrifice from all within the four seas;

sentences.zh: 言以義養，則仲由之菽，甘於東鄰之牲。

translations.literal: speaking of providing by righteousness, then Zhong You's beans were sweeter than the eastern neighbor's victims.
translations.idiomatic: and one who feeds parents by right principle proves that Zhong You's humble beans outshine the fat cattle of the house next door.

sentences.zh: [七]夫患水菽之薄，干祿以求養者，是以恥祿親也。

translations.literal: [Note 7] Now those who fret at the thinness of beans and water, who seek salary by importuning to obtain provision—thereby they shame salary before parents.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 7] To resent a spare diet of grain and water while scrambling for office just to feed parents is to make one's salary a reproach to one's kin.

sentences.zh: [八]存誠以盡行，孝積而祿厚者，此能以義養也。

translations.literal: [Note 8] To preserve sincerity so as to exhaust conduct, filial piety accumulated and salary thick—this is able to provide by righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 8] Sincerity that fills one's actions, until devotion deepens and one's stipend grows ample—that is providing by moral principle.

sentences.zh: 注[一]配天謂宗祀文王於明堂，以配上帝。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Matching Heaven means enshrining King Wen in the Bright Hall to match the Supreme Lord.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: "Matching Heaven" refers to sacrificing to King Wen in the Bright Hall as counterpart to the Supreme God.

sentences.zh: 注[二]事見禮記。

translations.literal: Comment [2] The matter is seen in the Record of Rites.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: See the *Book of Rites*.

sentences.zh: 啜音昌悅反。

translations.literal: Chuo is read chuo with initial ch- and final -uet (fanqie).
translations.idiomatic: The graph chuo is read chuo (fanqie spelling given in the commentary).

sentences.zh: 廣雅曰：「啜，食也。」

translations.literal: The Expanded Elegances says: "Chuo means eat."
translations.idiomatic: The *Guangya* defines chuo as "to eat."

sentences.zh: 注[三]論語孔子曰：「樂雲樂雲，鐘鼓雲乎哉？

translations.literal: Comment [3] The Analects—Confucius said: "Music, music—does it mean bells and drums?"
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: Confucius said in the *Analects*, "They speak of music—is it only bells and drums?"

sentences.zh: 」言樂之所貴者，移風易俗也，非謂鐘鼓而已，然而不可去鐘鼓。

translations.literal: " That what music esteems is transforming customs—does not mean only bells and drums, yet one cannot remove bells and drums.
translations.idiomatic: Music is prized for reforming manners and morals, not merely for percussion—but percussion remains indispensable.

sentences.zh: 去音丘呂反。

translations.literal: Qu (remove) is read qu with initial kh- and final -ü (fanqie).
translations.idiomatic: The graph qu meaning 'remove' is read with the fanqie spelling given (khü).

sentences.zh: 注[四]孝經曰：「雖日用三牲，猶為不孝。

translations.literal: Comment [4] The Classic of Filial Piety says: "Though one daily use the three sacrificial beasts, still it is unfilial."
translations.idiomatic: Note 4: The *Classic of Filial Piety* says, "Even daily offering the three sacrificial animals can be unfilial."

sentences.zh: 」言孝子者，以和顏悅色為難也，非謂三牲而已，然不可闕甘旨。

translations.literal: " That for a filial son, a kindly countenance and pleased color is the hard part—does not mean only the three beasts, yet one cannot lack sweet and fine food.
translations.idiomatic: The point is that a pleasant demeanor is the true test, not the meat alone—yet one should still try to set good food on the table.

sentences.zh: 注[五]遁，失也。

translations.literal: Comment [5] Dun means loss.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5: Here the graph glossed dun means 'to lose.'

sentences.zh: 言盛飾鐘廣之器而忘移風之本，是失樂之意也。

translations.literal: It means richly adorning bell-and-wide instruments and forgetting the root of transforming customs—this is losing music's meaning.
translations.idiomatic: To lavish decoration on instruments while forgetting that music should change customs is to miss the whole purpose of music.

sentences.zh: 注[六]不義而崇養，更為親憂，是孝之累也。

translations.literal: Comment [6] Unrighteous yet exalting provision, further becoming worry for parents—this is filial piety's burden.
translations.idiomatic: Note 6: Supporting parents through wrongdoing only adds to their worries—that is how filial duty becomes a burden.

sentences.zh: 注[七]易曰「東鄰殺牛，不如西鄰之禴祭」也。

translations.literal: Comment [7] The Changes says "The eastern neighbor slaughters an ox—is not as good as the western neighbor's yue sacrifice."
translations.idiomatic: Note 7: The *Book of Changes*: "The eastern neighbor kills an ox; it does not match the western neighbor's lean spring sacrifice."

sentences.zh: 注[八]干，求也。

translations.literal: Comment [8] Gan means seek.
translations.idiomatic: Note 8: Here the graph glossed gan means 'to seek.'

sentences.zh: 謂不以道求祿，故可恥也。

translations.literal: It means not by the Way seeking salary; therefore it is shameful.
translations.idiomatic: Seeking office by improper means is what brings shame.

sentences.zh: 中興，廬江毛義少節，家貧，以孝行稱。

translations.literal: At the restoration, Mao Yi of Lujiang in youth had integrity; the family was poor; he was praised for filial conduct.
translations.idiomatic: After the restoration, Mao Yi of Lujiang was poor but principled and famed for filial piety.

sentences.zh: 南陽人張奉慕其名，往候之。

translations.literal: Zhang Feng of Nanyang admired his name and went to visit him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Feng of Nanyang, admiring his reputation, called on him.

sentences.zh: 坐定而府檄適至，以義守令，[一]義奉檄而入，喜動顏色。

translations.literal: When they had sat and settled, a commandery dispatch arrived to appoint Yi acting magistrate; [Note 1] Yi took the dispatch and entered, joy moving his expression.
translations.idiomatic: They had barely sat down when a summons arrived naming Mao Yi acting magistrate. He stepped inside with the document, joy written on his face.

sentences.zh: 奉者，志尚士也，心賤之，自恨來，固辭而去。

translations.literal: Feng was a man of lofty aims; in his heart he despised him, regretted having come, firmly declined and departed.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Feng was a man who prized high ideals; he despised Mao Yi's reaction, regretted his visit, and left after a curt farewell.

sentences.zh: 及義母死，去官行服。

translations.literal: When Yi's mother died, he left office to observe mourning.
translations.idiomatic: When Mao Yi's mother died, he resigned to mourn.

sentences.zh: 數辟公府，為縣令，進退必以禮。

translations.literal: Several times he was summoned by the duke's offices; he became county magistrate; advancing and withdrawing were always by ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Repeatedly summoned to high office and serving as county magistrate, he advanced or withdrew only as ritual prescribed.

sentences.zh: 後舉賢良，公車征，遂不至。

translations.literal: Later he was recommended worthy and good; the imperial carriage summoned him; he finally did not arrive.
translations.idiomatic: Later recommended as worthy and excellent, he was summoned by imperial coach—and stayed home.

sentences.zh: 張奉歎曰：「賢者固不可測。

translations.literal: Zhang Feng sighed and said: "The worthy indeed cannot be fathomed.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Feng sighed, "A worthy man is never easy to read.

sentences.zh: 往日之喜，乃為親屈也。

translations.literal: The joy of other days was thus bending for parents.
translations.idiomatic: His joy over the summons had been the relief of winning a salary for his mother.

sentences.zh: 斯蓋所謂『家貧親老，不擇官而仕』者也。

translations.literal: This is perhaps what is called 'when the family is poor and parents old, one does not choose the office but serves.'"
translations.idiomatic: That is what men mean when they say, 'When the family is poor and parents are old, take whatever office you can get.'"

sentences.zh: 」[二]建初中，章帝下詔□寵義，賜谷千斛，常以八月長吏問起居，加賜羊酒。

translations.literal: " [Note 2] In the Jianchu period, Emperor Zhang issued an edict [praising] and honoring Yi, granted a thousand hu of grain, regularly in the eighth month senior clerks inquired after his health, additionally granted sheep and wine.
translations.idiomatic: " [Note 2] During Jianchu, Emperor Zhang issued an edict honoring Mao Yi, sent a thousand hu of grain, ordered local officials each eighth month to inquire after him, and added gifts of mutton and wine.

sentences.zh: 壽終於家。

translations.literal: He completed his lifespan at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home of old age.

sentences.zh: 注[一]檄，召書也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Xi is a summons document.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The term denotes an official summons document.

sentences.zh: 東觀記曰：「義為安陽尉，府檄到，當守令」也。

translations.literal: The Eastern Lodge Record says: "Yi was marshal of Anyang; when the commandery dispatch arrived, he was to act as magistrate."
translations.idiomatic: The *Eastern Lodge Han Records* notes that Mao Yi was sheriff of Anyang and was ordered by dispatch to serve as acting magistrate.

sentences.zh: 注[二]韓詩外傳曾子曰：「任重道遠，不擇地而息。

translations.literal: [Note 2] The Outer Commentary on the Han Odes—Master Zeng said: "The burden is heavy and the road long; one does not choose the place to rest.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Master Zeng in the *Han Shi Wai Zhuan* says, "The burden is heavy and the road is long; one does not pick where to rest.

sentences.zh: 家貧親老，不擇官而仕。」

translations.literal: When the family is poor and parents old, one does not choose the office but serves."
translations.idiomatic: When the family is poor and parents are aged, one takes whatever office comes."

sentences.zh: 安帝時，汝南薛包孟嘗，好學篤行，喪母，以至孝聞。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor An, Xue Bao Mengchang of Runan loved learning and practiced earnestly; his mother died; he was heard of for utmost filial piety.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor An, Xue Bao (courtesy Mengchang) of Runan was studious and conscientious. After his mother's death he became famous for extraordinary devotion.

sentences.zh: 及父娶後妻而憎包，分出之，包日夜號泣，不能去，至被歐杖。

translations.literal: When his father married a second wife and hated Bao, he divided out Bao; Bao day and night wailed and wept, could not leave, until beaten with clubs.
translations.idiomatic: When his father remarried and turned against him, the family cast him out. He wept night and day and refused to go until they beat him.

sentences.zh: 不得已，廬於捨外，旦入而灑掃，父怒，又逐之。

translations.literal: Unable to avoid it, he built a hut outside the compound; each dawn he entered and sprinkled and swept; his father was angry and again drove him.
translations.idiomatic: Forced out, he lived in a hut beside the house and still swept the yard each morning until his father drove him off again.

sentences.zh: 乃廬於里門，昏晨不廢。

translations.literal: Thereupon he built a hut at the village gate; dusk and dawn he did not cease.
translations.idiomatic: He moved his hut to the village gate and morning and evening never neglected his duties.

sentences.zh: 積歲餘，父母籩而還之。

translations.literal: After more than a year, his father and mother felt shame and returned him.
translations.idiomatic: After a year his parents relented and brought him home.

sentences.zh: 後行六年服，喪過乎哀。

translations.literal: Later he observed six-year mourning; mourning exceeded in grief.
translations.idiomatic: He later observed mourning beyond the norm, grieving longer than ritual required.

sentences.zh: 既而弟子求分財異居，包不能止，乃中分其財。

translations.literal: Afterward his younger brothers sought to divide property and live apart; Bao could not stop them; thereupon he divided the property in half.
translations.idiomatic: When his younger brothers demanded a split household, he could not refuse and divided the estate evenly.

sentences.zh: 奴婢引其老者，曰：「與我共事久，若不能使也。

translations.literal: Of bond servants he led forth the old ones, saying: "They have long worked with me—if you cannot employ them.
translations.idiomatic: He gave his brothers the older slaves, saying, "They have served me for years—you may find them hard to use."

sentences.zh: 」田廬取其荒頓者，[一]曰：「吾少時所理，意所戀也。

translations.literal: " Of fields and huts he took the waste and ruined ones; [Note 1] saying: "These I ordered in my youth—what my heart is attached to.
translations.idiomatic: " He took the worn-out fields and sheds, saying, "I laid these out long ago and cannot bear to leave them."

sentences.zh: 」器物取朽敗者，曰：「我素所服食，身口所安也。

translations.literal: " Of utensils he took the rotten and ruined, saying: "These I have always eaten and worn—what body and mouth are at ease with.
translations.idiomatic: " He kept the broken tools, saying, "These are what my hands and mouth have grown used to."

sentences.zh: 」弟子數破其產，輒復賑給。

translations.literal: " The younger brothers several times ruined his property; he always again relieved and supplied.
translations.idiomatic: " Whenever his brothers squandered their shares, he fed and clothed them again.

sentences.zh: 建光中，公車特徵，至，拜侍中。

translations.literal: In the Jianguang period, the imperial carriage specially summoned him; when he arrived, he was appointed palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: During Jianguang he received a special summons and was named palace attendant on arrival.

sentences.zh: 包性恬虛，稱疾不起，以死自乞。

translations.literal: Bao by nature was tranquil and empty; he claimed illness and did not rise; by death begged himself.
translations.idiomatic: Xue Bao was retiring by nature; he pleaded illness and begged to be spared office even at the cost of his life.

sentences.zh: 有詔賜告歸，加禮如毛義。

translations.literal: There was an edict granting him leave to return home; additional ritual like Mao Yi.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor granted him sick leave to go home with honors matching Mao Yi's.

sentences.zh: [二]年八十餘，以壽終。

translations.literal: [Note 2] Age eighty-something; he ended by longevity.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 2] He lived past eighty and died at home.

sentences.zh: 注[一]頓猶廢也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Dun here means ruined.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Here dun means 'ruined' or 'derelict.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]告，請假也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Gao means requesting leave.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Here gao means requesting administrative leave.

sentences.zh: 漢制，吏病滿三月當免，天下優賜其告，使得帶印綬，將官屬，歸家養病，謂之賜告也。

translations.literal: Han institution: when an official's illness fills three months he ought be dismissed; the realm graciously grants his leave, allowing him to bear seal-ribbon, lead official subordinates, return home and nurture illness—this is called granted leave.
translations.idiomatic: Han law dismissed officials sick three months, but the throne could "grant leave," letting them keep rank and staff while recuperating at home.

sentences.zh: 若二子者，推至誠以為行，行信於心而感於人，以成名受祿致禮，斯可謂能以孝養也。

translations.literal: As for these two men, they extended utmost sincerity to make conduct; conduct trusted in heart and moved others; thereby achieving name, receiving salary, bringing ritual—these can be called able to provide by filial piety.
translations.idiomatic: These two acted from utter sincerity: inward conviction that touched others, earning fame, stipends, and imperial favor—true provision born of filial duty.

sentences.zh: 若夫江革、劉般數公者之義行，猶斯志也。

translations.literal: As for Jiang Ge, Liu Ban, and several gentlemen's righteous conduct—it is still this intent.
translations.idiomatic: Jiang Ge, Liu Ban, and the others in this chapter shared the same resolve.

sentences.zh: 撰其行事著於篇。

translations.literal: Selected their deeds and set them in the chapter.
translations.idiomatic: Their deeds are recorded here.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]自此已上，並略華嶠之詞也。

translations.literal: [Note 1] Comment [1] From this upward, all summarizes Hua Qiao's wording.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] The foregoing condenses Hua Qiao's original text.

sentences.zh: 劉平字公子，楚郡彭城人也。

translations.literal: Liu Ping, courtesy name Gongzi, was a man of Pengcheng in Chu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ping, styled Gongzi, came from Pengcheng in the Chu princedom.

sentences.zh: 本名曠，顯宗後改為平。

translations.literal: His original name was Kuang; after Emperor Xianzong it was changed to Ping.
translations.idiomatic: Born Liu Kuang, he adopted the name Ping after Emperor Ming's reign.

sentences.zh: 王莽時為郡吏，守菑丘長，[一]政教大行。

translations.literal: In Wang Mang's time he was a commandery clerk, guarded as magistrate of Zaiqiu; [Note 1] governance and teaching greatly flourished.
translations.idiomatic: Under Wang Mang he served the commandery as acting magistrate of Zaiqiu, where his civil instruction flourished.

sentences.zh: 其後每屬縣有劇賊，輒令平守之，所至皆理，由是一郡稱其能。

translations.literal: Afterward whenever a subordinate county had fierce bandits, they always ordered Ping to guard it; wherever he went all was ordered—thereby the whole commandery praised his ability.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever a county faced serious banditry, the commandery sent Liu Ping; he restored order each time and earned a reputation for competence.

sentences.zh: 注[一]菑丘，縣，屬彭城國。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Zaiqiu is a county belonging to the Pengcheng state.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Zaiqiu county lay in the Pengcheng kingdom.

sentences.zh: 更始時，天下亂，平弟仲為賊所殺。

translations.literal: In Gengshi's time the realm was in chaos; Ping's younger brother Zhong was killed by bandits.
translations.idiomatic: During the Gengshi interregnum his brother Liu Zhong fell to bandits.

sentences.zh: 其後賊復忽然而至，平扶侍其母，奔走逃難。

translations.literal: Afterward bandits again suddenly arrived; Ping supported and attended his mother, ran and fled hardship.
translations.idiomatic: When raiders struck again, he fled with his mother.

sentences.zh: 仲遺腹女始一歲，平抱仲女而□其子。

translations.literal: Zhong's posthumous daughter had just reached one year; Ping embraced Zhong's daughter and [abandoned] his own son.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong left an infant daughter; Liu Ping carried the girl and abandoned his own boy.

sentences.zh: 母欲還取之，平不聽，曰：「力不能兩活，仲不可以絕類。

translations.literal: His mother wished to return and fetch him; Ping would not listen, saying: "Strength cannot keep both alive—Zhong cannot be cut off from posterity.
translations.idiomatic: His mother wanted to go back for the child; he refused. "We cannot save both," he said; "Zhong's line must not end."

sentences.zh: 」遂去不顧，與母俱匿野澤中。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he left without looking back, with his mother both hid in wild marsh.
translations.idiomatic: " They pressed on and hid with his mother in the wetlands.

sentences.zh: 平朝出求食，逢餓賊，將亨*[之]*，平叩頭曰：「今旦為老母求菜，老母待曠為命，願得先歸，食母異，還就死。」

translations.literal: Ping in the morning went out seeking food; met starving bandits; they were about to boil him; Ping knocked his head saying: "This dawn I sought greens for my old mother—my old mother waits on Kuang for life—I beg to return first, feed my mother differently, then come back to die."
translations.idiomatic: One morning he went for food and starving rebels seized him for the pot. He kowtowed: "I was out gathering greens for my mother; she depends on me to live. Let me go back and feed her first; once she has eaten, I will return and die."

sentences.zh: [一]因涕泣。

translations.literal: [Note 1] Thereupon he wept.
translations.idiomatic: He wept as he spoke.

sentences.zh: 賊見其至誠，哀而遣之。

translations.literal: The bandits saw his utmost sincerity, pitied him, and sent him off.
translations.idiomatic: Touched by his earnestness, the bandits let him go.

sentences.zh: 平還，既食母訖，因白曰：「屬與賊期，義不可欺。

translations.literal: Ping returned; having finished feeding his mother, he thereupon stated: "I have just now made a tryst with the bandits—righteousness may not deceive.
translations.idiomatic: He returned, fed his mother, and said, "I gave the raiders my word; honor forbids a lie."

sentences.zh: 」遂還詣賊。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he returned and went to the bandits.
translations.idiomatic: " Then he went back to the enemy camp.

sentences.zh: 觿皆大驚，相謂曰：「常聞烈士，乃今見之。

translations.literal: The young men all greatly marveled, saying to one another: "We always hear of a man of ardor—now we see one.
translations.idiomatic: The gang gaped at one another: "We had heard of steadfast heroes—today we meet one."

sentences.zh: 子去矣，吾不忍食子。

translations.literal: Sir, go—we cannot bear to eat you.
translations.idiomatic: Go, sir—we cannot eat a man like you.

sentences.zh: 」於是得全。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he was wholly preserved.
translations.idiomatic: " Thus he lived.

sentences.zh: 注[一]食音飼。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Shi is read like si 'feed.'
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Here shi is read like si, meaning 'to feed.'

sentences.zh: 下同。

translations.literal: Below same.
translations.idiomatic: Same gloss applies below.

sentences.zh: 建武初，平狄將軍龐萌反於彭城，攻敗郡守孫萌。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Jianwu, General Who Pacifies the Di Pang Meng rebelled at Pengcheng, attacked and defeated Grand Administrator Sun Meng.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Jianwu, Pang Meng, general who pacifies the Di, rebelled at Pengcheng and defeated Prefect Sun Meng.

sentences.zh: 平時復為郡吏，冒白刃伏萌身上，被七創，困頓不知所為，號泣請曰：「願以身代府君。

translations.literal: At the time Ping again was a commandery clerk; braving bare blades he lay prone on Meng's body, suffered seven wounds, exhausted did not know what to do, wailed and wept requesting: "I wish to substitute my body for the lord of the office.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ping, again a commandery clerk, threw himself over Sun Meng under the rebels' blades, took seven wounds, and sobbed, "Kill me instead of the prefect."

sentences.zh: 」賊乃斂兵止，曰：「此義士也，勿殺。

translations.literal: " The bandits thereupon gathered weapons and stopped, saying: "This is a righteous knight—do not kill.
translations.idiomatic: " The rebels lowered their weapons: "This is a man of honor—let him be."

sentences.zh: 」遂解去。

translations.literal: " Thereupon they released and departed.
translations.idiomatic: " They withdrew.

sentences.zh: 萌傷甚氣絕，有頃蘇，渴求飲。

translations.literal: Meng's wounds were severe; breath stopped; after a moment he revived, thirstily sought drink.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Meng seemed dead from his wounds, then stirred and begged for water.

sentences.zh: 平傾其創血以飲之。

translations.literal: Ping poured his wound blood to give him to drink.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ping trickled blood from his own wounds into Sun Meng's mouth.

sentences.zh: 後數日萌竟死，平乃裹創，扶送萌喪，至其本縣。

translations.literal: After several days Meng finally died; Ping thereupon bound his wounds, helped escort Meng's funeral, reaching his home county.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Meng died a few days later; Liu Ping dressed his wounds and escorted the coffin to Sun's home county.

sentences.zh: 後舉孝廉，拜濟陰郡丞，太守劉育甚重之，任以郡職，上書薦平。

translations.literal: Later he was recommended Filially Pious and Incorrupt; appointed assistant to the grand administrator of Jiyin; Grand Administrator Liu Yu greatly esteemed him, entrusted him with commandery duties, submitted memorial recommending Ping.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended as filially pious and incorrupt, he became aide to the governor of Jiyin; Liu Yu trusted him with command affairs and memorialized his merits.

sentences.zh: 會平遭父喪去官。

translations.literal: It happened Ping met father's mourning and left office.
translations.idiomatic: He resigned when his father died.

sentences.zh: 服闋，拜全椒長，[一]政有恩惠，百姓懷感，人或增貲就賦，或減年從役。

translations.literal: When mourning was ended, he was appointed magistrate of Quanjiao; [Note 1] governance had favor and kindness; the hundred surnames cherished gratitude—some increased goods to pay tax, some reduced years to follow labor.
translations.idiomatic: After mourning he became magistrate of Quanjiao, ruling with kindness so that people voluntarily raised their tax assessments or shortened their labor terms.

sentences.zh: 刺史、太守行部，獄無系囚，人自以得所，不知所問，[二]唯班詔書而去。

translations.literal: When the governor and grand administrator toured the department, the prison had no bound prisoners; people each thought they had obtained their place—did not know what to ask; [Note 2] only distributed the edict and departed.
translations.idiomatic: When inspectors visited, the jails stood empty and every household felt fairly treated—officials had nothing to investigate beyond proclaiming the imperial edict and leaving.

sentences.zh: 後以病免。

translations.literal: Later because of illness he was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Ill health eventually forced him from office.

sentences.zh: 注[一]全椒，縣，屬九江郡也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Quanjiao is a county belonging to Jiujiang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Quanjiao county was part of Jiujiang commandery.

sentences.zh: 注[二]「所」或作「何」。

translations.literal: Comment [2] "Where" is sometimes written "what."
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Some texts read "what" instead of "where."

sentences.zh: 顯宗初，尚書僕射鐘離意上書薦平及琅邪王望、東萊王扶曰：「臣竊見琅邪王望、楚國劉曠、東萊王扶，皆年七十，執性恬淡，所居之處，邑裡化之，修身行義，應在朝次。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Emperor Xianzong, Deputy Director of the Secretariat Zhongli Yi submitted a memorial recommending Ping together with Wang Wang of Langya and Wang Fu of Donglai, saying: "Your servant has privately seen Wang Wang of Langya, Liu Kuang of Chu, and Wang Fu of Donglai—all age seventy, holding nature tranquil and plain; wherever they dwell, towns and villages are transformed by them; they cultivate body and practice righteousness—they ought to be in court rank.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Ming's reign, Zhongli Yi, deputy director of the Secretariat, memorialized for Liu Ping together with Wang Wang of Langya and Wang Fu of Donglai: "These three—Wang Wang, Liu Kuang, and Wang Fu—are seventy years old, retiring by temperament, and everywhere they live their neighborhoods take their moral cue; they cultivate themselves and live by principle and belong at court."

sentences.zh: 臣誠不足知人，竊慕推士進賢之義。

translations.literal: Your servant truly is insufficient to know men; I privately admire the righteousness of recommending gentlemen and advancing the worthy.
translations.idiomatic: I am no judge of character, yet I wish to honor the duty of lifting worthy men into office."

sentences.zh: 」書奏，有詔征平等，特賜辦裝錢。

translations.literal: " When the memorial was submitted, there was an edict summoning Ping and the others; specially granted funds for travel outfit.
translations.idiomatic: " The emperor summoned them and granted travel money.

sentences.zh: 至皆拜議郎，並數引見。

translations.literal: When they arrived, all were appointed gentlemen consultants; together they were frequently granted audiences.
translations.idiomatic: They reached the capital and were named gentlemen consultants with repeated imperial audiences.

sentences.zh: 平再遷侍中，永平三年，拜宗正，數薦達名士承宮、郇恁等。

translations.literal: Ping was again promoted palace attendant; in the third year of Yongping, appointed supervisor of the imperial clan; several times recommended and advanced noted gentlemen Cheng Gong, Xun Ren, and others.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ping rose to palace attendant and in Yongping 3 became supervisor of the imperial clan, repeatedly advancing scholars such as Cheng Gong and Xun Ren.

sentences.zh: [一]在位八年，以老病上疏乞骸骨，卒於家。

translations.literal: [Note 1] In office eight years; because of old age and illness submitted memorial begging old bones; died at home.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] After eight years he cited age and illness, retired, and died at home.

sentences.zh: 注[一]恁字君大，見黃憲傳。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Ren's courtesy name is Junda—see the biography of Huang Xian.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Xun Ren, styled Junda, appears in the biography of Huang Xian.

sentences.zh: 恁音人甚反。

translations.literal: Ren is read with initial r- and final -im (fanqie).
translations.idiomatic: Ren is glossed with the fanqie spelling given in the commentary.

sentences.zh: 王望字慈卿，客授會稽，自議郎遷青州刺史，甚有威名。

translations.literal: Wang Wang, courtesy name Ciqing, as guest instructed Kuaiji; from gentleman consultant was promoted governor of Qingzhou—he had a very awe-inspiring reputation.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Wang, styled Ciqing, taught privately in Kuaiji before rising from gentleman consultant to inspector of Qingzhou, where he earned wide respect.

sentences.zh: 是時州郡災旱，百姓窮荒，望行部，道見饑者，裸行草食，五百餘人，愍然哀之，因以便宜出所在布粟，給其*

translations.literal: At this time provinces and commanderies suffered disaster drought; the hundred surnames were destitute and wasted; Wang toured his department; on the road he saw starving people, naked walking eating grass—more than five hundred; he pitied and pitied them; therefore by expedient authority issued local cloth and grain for relief.
translations.idiomatic: Drought had ruined the region. On inspection Wang saw more than five hundred starving people stripped naked and eating weeds; moved with pity, he used emergency powers to disburse local grain stores.

translations.literal: (granary)
translations.idiomatic: Drawn from the state granaries.

sentences.zh: **[稟]*糧，為作褐衣。

translations.literal: grain ration, for them made hemp clothing.
translations.idiomatic: He issued grain and had rough hemp garments made for them.

sentences.zh: [一]事畢上言，帝以望不先表請，章示百官，詳議其罪。

translations.literal: [Note 1] When the affair was finished he reported upward; the emperor because Wang had not first memorialized for permission, displayed the memorial to the hundred officials, deliberated in detail his crime.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] Afterward he reported his action; the emperor, angry that Wang had not asked first, circulated the case to the court for judgment.

sentences.zh: 時公卿皆以為望之專命，法有常條。

translations.literal: At the time dukes and ministers all thought Wang's acting on his own authority—the law has constant articles.
translations.idiomatic: The ministers held that Wang had usurped authority and that statute prescribed a penalty.

sentences.zh: 鐘離意獨曰：「昔華元、子反，楚、宋之良臣，不稟君命，□平二國，春秋之義，以為美談。

translations.literal: Zhongli Yi alone said: "Formerly Hua Yuan and Zifan, good ministers of Chu and Song, did not receive lord's orders—[yet] pacified two states; the Spring and Autumn's righteousness took them as fine tales.
translations.idiomatic: Only Zhongli Yi objected: "Hua Yuan and Sima Zifan once ended a Chu–Song war without orders; the *Spring and Autumn* praises them."

sentences.zh: [二]今望懷義忘罪，當仁不讓，若繩之以法，忽其本情，將乖聖朝愛育之旨。

translations.literal: [Note 2] Now Wang harbors righteousness and forgets crime; meets benevolence and does not yield—if we rope him with law, neglect his basic feeling, will violate the sagely court's intent of loving nurture.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 2] Wang acted from humanity; to punish him without weighing motive would betray the throne's ideal of compassion."

sentences.zh: 」帝嘉意議，赦而不罪。

translations.literal: " The emperor praised Yi's deliberation, pardoned and did not punish.
translations.idiomatic: " Emperor Ming accepted Zhongli Yi's view and pardoned Wang.

sentences.zh: 注[一]許慎注淮南子曰：「楚人謂袍為短褐。」

translations.literal: Comment [1] Xu Shen's commentary on Huainanzi says: "Chu people call a robe short hemp."
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Xu Shen notes that in Chu a short robe was called a "short hemp" garment.

sentences.zh: 注[二]春秋：「楚子圍宋，宋人及楚人平。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Spring and Autumn: "The viscount of Chu besieged Song; men of Song made peace with men of Chu."
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The *Spring and Autumn*: "The lord of Chu besieged Song until Song sued for peace."

sentences.zh: 」公羊傳曰：「外平不書，此何以書？

translations.literal: " The Gongyang commentary says: "Peace made with outsiders is not recorded—why is this recorded?
translations.idiomatic: " The *Gongyang* asks why this peace treaty was recorded when others were not.

sentences.zh: 大其平乎己也。

translations.literal: They magnify the peace as coming from oneself.
translations.idiomatic: Because the peace derived from their own initiative.

sentences.zh: 何大其平乎己？

translations.literal: Why magnify the peace as coming from oneself?
translations.idiomatic: Why praise peace born of their own judgment?

sentences.zh: 莊王圍宋，有七日之糧爾，盡此不勝，將去而歸爾，於是使司馬子反乘堙而窺宋城，宋華元亦乘堙而出見之。

translations.literal: King Zhuang besieged Song, had only seven days' grain—if this exhausted without victory, would leave and return; thereupon ordered Minister of War Zifan ride the mound and spy Song city; Song's Hua Yuan also rode the mound and came out to see him.
translations.idiomatic: King Zhuang of Chu had laid siege to Song with only a week's provisions; he sent Sima Zifan up the rampart to spy, and Hua Yuan of Song came out to parley.

sentences.zh: 子反曰：『子之國何如？

translations.literal: Zifan said: 'How is your state?
translations.idiomatic: Zifan asked, "How fares your city?"

sentences.zh: 』華元曰：『憊矣。

translations.literal: ' Hua Yuan said: 'We are exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: " "Ruined," said Hua Yuan.

sentences.zh: 』曰：『何如？

translations.literal: ' He said: 'How?
translations.idiomatic: " "In what way?"

sentences.zh: 』曰：『易子而食之，析骸而炊之。

translations.literal: ' He said: 'We exchange children and eat them; split bones and cook them.
translations.idiomatic: " "We swap children for the pot and burn bones for fuel."

sentences.zh: 』子反曰：『諾。

translations.literal: ' Zifan said: 'Yes.
translations.idiomatic: " "I understand," said Zifan.

sentences.zh: 吾軍有七日之糧爾。

translations.literal: Our army has only seven days' grain.
translations.idiomatic: Our army too has only seven days of grain.

sentences.zh: 盡此不勝，將去而歸爾。

translations.literal: If this exhausts without victory, will leave and return.
translations.idiomatic: When it is gone we march home.

sentences.zh: 』揖而去之，反於莊王。

translations.literal: ' Bowed and left him, returned to King Zhuang.
translations.idiomatic: " He bowed and reported to King Zhuang.

sentences.zh: 莊王怒曰：『吾使子往視之，子曷為告之！

translations.literal: King Zhuang angrily said: 'I sent you to inspect—why did you tell him!
translations.idiomatic: " "I sent you to scout," raged the king; "why did you brief him?"

sentences.zh: 』子反曰：『以區區之宋，猶有不欺人之臣，可以楚而無乎？

translations.literal: ' Zifan said: 'For tiny Song there are still ministers who do not deceive—can Chu lack them?
translations.idiomatic: " "Tiny Song still has honest ministers," Zifan replied; "can mighty Chu do less?"

sentences.zh: 是以告之。

translations.literal: Therefore I told him.
translations.idiomatic: So I told him the truth."

sentences.zh: 』王曰：『諾。

translations.literal: ' The king said: 'Yes.
translations.idiomatic: " "Very well," said the king.

sentences.zh: 』引師而去之。

translations.literal: ' Led the army and left it.
translations.idiomatic: " He lifted the siege and withdrew.

sentences.zh: 故君子大其平乎己也。」

translations.literal: Therefore the noble man magnifies the peace as coming from oneself."
translations.idiomatic: Hence the classic praises peace achieved by the ministers' own judgment."

sentences.zh: 王扶字子元，掖人也。

translations.literal: Wang Fu, courtesy name Ziyuan, was a man of Ye.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Fu, styled Ziyuan, came from Ye county.

sentences.zh: [一]少修節行，客居琅邪不其縣，所止聚落化其德。

translations.literal: [Note 1] In youth he cultivated integrity and conduct; as guest dwelt in Buqi county of Langya; wherever he stopped, hamlets were transformed by his virtue.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] In youth he cultivated himself while living in Buqi in Langya; every hamlet he touched absorbed his virtue.

sentences.zh: [二] 國相張宗謁請，不應，欲強致之，遂杖策歸鄉里。

translations.literal: [Note 2] Chancellor Zhang Zong visited and requested; he did not respond; wished forcibly to bring him—thereupon staff-in-hand returned to village.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 2] Chancellor Zhang Zong wanted to press him into service; Wang Fu refused and walked home with staff in hand.

sentences.zh: 連請，固病不起。

translations.literal: Repeated requests—firmly ill did not rise.
translations.idiomatic: Further summonses met pleas of illness.

sentences.zh: 太傅鄧禹辟，不至。

translations.literal: Grand Tutor Deng Yu summoned—did not arrive.
translations.idiomatic: Even Grand Tutor Deng Yu could not bring him out.

sentences.zh: 後拜議郎，會見，恂恂似不能言。

translations.literal: Later appointed gentleman consultant; at audience, deferential as if unable to speak.
translations.idiomatic: Named gentleman consultant at last, he seemed timid in audience and barely spoke.

sentences.zh: [三]然性沉正，不可干以非義，當世高之。

translations.literal: [Note 3] Yet his nature was grave and upright; could not be importuned with unrighteousness—his age esteemed him.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 3] Yet he was grave and incorruptible; his contemporaries revered him.

sentences.zh: 永平中，臨邑侯劉復[四]著漢德頌，盛稱扶為名臣雲。

translations.literal: In Yongping, Marquis of Linyi Liu Fu [Note 4] composed Ode to Han Virtue, greatly praising Fu as a noted minister.
translations.idiomatic: During Yongping, Liu Fu of Linyi praised Wang Fu as a model minister in his *Ode to Han Virtue*.

sentences.zh: 注[一]掖，今萊州縣。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Ye—now Laizhou county.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Ye is in modern Laizhou.

sentences.zh: 注[二]小於鄉曰聚。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Smaller than a township is called a hamlet.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: A settlement smaller than a township is a hamlet.

sentences.zh: 廣雅曰：「落，居也。」

translations.literal: The Expanded Elegances says: "Luo means dwelling."
translations.idiomatic: The *Guangya* glosses luo as "dwelling place."

sentences.zh: 注[三]恂恂，恭順之魍。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Xunxun—deferential appearance.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: Xunxun describes a deferential manner (text may read mao for appearance).

sentences.zh: 注[四]復，光武兄伯升之孫，北海王興之子也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] Fu is grandson of Guangwu's elder brother Bosheng, son of Prince Xing of Beihai.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4: Liu Fu was grandson of Liu Bosheng and son of Prince Xing of Beihai.

sentences.zh: 趙孝字長平，沛國蘄人也。

translations.literal: Zhao Xiao, courtesy name Changping, was a man of Qi in the Pei kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Xiao, styled Changping, came from Qi in the kingdom of Pei.

sentences.zh: [一]父普，王莽時為田禾將軍，[二]任孝為郎。

translations.literal: [Note 1] His father Pu, in Wang Mang's time was General of Field and Grain; [Note 2] appointed Xiao gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] His father Zhao Pu was General of Field and Grain under Wang Mang. [Note 2] He secured Zhao Xiao a post as gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 每告歸，常白衣步擔。

translations.literal: Whenever granted leave to return, always in white clothes walked carrying on shoulder pole.
translations.idiomatic: On leave he traveled as a commoner in plain cloth, walking with a shoulder pole.

sentences.zh: 嘗從長安還，欲止郵亭。

translations.literal: Once returning from Chang'an, wished to stop at a post station.
translations.idiomatic: Returning once from Chang'an, he tried to spend the night at a postal hostel.

sentences.zh: 亭長先時聞孝當過，以有長者客，掃灑待之。

translations.literal: The station chief beforehand heard Xiao would pass; because there was an elder guest, swept and sprinkled awaiting him.
translations.idiomatic: The hosteller knew a distinguished visitor named Zhao was coming and cleaned the place for him.

sentences.zh: [三]孝既至，不自名，[四]長不肯內，因問曰：「聞田禾將軍子當從長安來，何時至乎？

translations.literal: [Note 3] When Xiao arrived, he did not state his name; [Note 4] the chief would not admit him; therefore asked saying: "I heard the Field-and-Grain general's son ought to come from Chang'an—when does he arrive?
translations.idiomatic: [Note 3] Zhao Xiao gave no name. [Note 4] The hosteller barred him and asked when the general's son would arrive from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 」孝曰：「尋到矣。

translations.literal: " Xiao said: "He will arrive presently.
translations.idiomatic: " "Very soon," said Zhao Xiao.

sentences.zh: 」於是遂去。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he departed.
translations.idiomatic: " And he walked on.

sentences.zh: [五]及天下亂，人相食。

translations.literal: [Note 5] When the realm fell into chaos, people ate each other.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 5] When the empire collapsed into famine, men preyed on one another.

sentences.zh: 孝弟禮為餓賊所得，孝聞之，即自縛詣賊，曰：「禮久餓羸瘦，不如孝肥飽。

translations.literal: Xiao's younger brother Li was seized by starving bandits; Xiao heard it, immediately bound himself and went to the bandits, saying: "Li long starving is lean and wasted—not as good as Xiao fat and full.
translations.idiomatic: When bandits seized his brother Zhao Li, Zhao Xiao tied his own hands and offered himself: "My brother is wasted with hunger—eat me instead; I am fatter."

sentences.zh: 」賊大驚，並放之，謂曰：「可且歸，更持米糒來。

translations.literal: " The bandits greatly marveled, released both together, said to them: "You may for now return—again bring dry rice.
translations.idiomatic: " Astonished, they freed both brothers and said, "Go fetch rice cakes for us."

sentences.zh: 」孝求不能得，復往報賊，願就亨。

translations.literal: " Xiao sought but could not obtain; again went and reported to the bandits, wished to submit to boiling.
translations.idiomatic: " Unable to find food, he returned and offered himself to the pot again.

sentences.zh: 觿異之，遂不害。

translations.literal: The young men marveled at him; thereupon did not harm.
translations.idiomatic: They marveled and let him live.

sentences.zh: 鄉黨服其義。

translations.literal: Village and district submitted to his righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: His neighbors revered his conduct.

sentences.zh: 州郡辟召，進退必以禮。

translations.literal: Provinces and commanderies summoned; advancing and withdrawing were always by ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Provincial offices summoned him; he accepted or declined only as ritual allowed.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉，不應。

translations.literal: Recommended Filially Pious and Incorrupt—did not respond.
translations.idiomatic: He ignored recommendation as filially pious and incorrupt.

sentences.zh: 注[一]蘄音機。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Qi is read ji.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Qi is read ji.

sentences.zh: 注[二]王莽時置田禾將軍，屯田北邊。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Wang Mang's time established General of Field and Grain, garrison-field northern frontier.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Wang Mang created the title to oversee northern military colonies.

sentences.zh: 注[三]素聞孝高名，故以為長者客也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Always hearing Xiao's high name, therefore thought him elder guest.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: The hosteller expected the famous Zhao Xiao as his guest.

sentences.zh: 「灑」與「灑」通，音所買反。

translations.literal: "Sprinkle" is interchangeable with "sprinkle"; read suo mai fan.
translations.idiomatic: The variant graphs for "sprinkle" are interchangeable (reading note follows).

sentences.zh: 注[四]不稱名也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] Did not declare name.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4: He did not give his name.

sentences.zh: 注[五]華嶠書曰：「孝報雲三日至矣。」

translations.literal: Comment [5] Hua Qiao's book says: "Xiao reported saying three days and he will arrive."
translations.idiomatic: Note 5: Hua Qiao's history quotes Zhao Xiao as saying he would arrive in three days.

sentences.zh: 永平中，辟太尉府，顯宗素聞其行，詔拜諫議大夫，遷侍中，又遷長樂□尉。

translations.literal: In Yongping, summoned to grand commandant's office; Xianzong always heard of his conduct; edict appointed grandee remonstrant; promoted palace attendant; again promoted guard commandant of Changle palace.
translations.idiomatic: During Yongping he entered the grand commandant's staff; Emperor Ming, long admiring him, named him grandee remonstrant, then palace attendant, then capital guard commandant at Changle.

sentences.zh: 復征弟禮為御史中丞。

translations.literal: Again summoned younger brother Li as vice censor-in-chief.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Zhao Li was promoted to vice censor-in-chief.

sentences.zh: 禮亦恭謙行己，類於孝。

translations.literal: Li also was respectful and humble in conducting himself, similar to Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Li matched his brother in humility.

sentences.zh: 帝嘉其兄弟篤行，欲寵異之，詔禮十日一就□尉府，太官送供具，令共相對盡歡。

translations.literal: The emperor praised their brothers' earnest conduct, wished to favor them specially; edict ordered Li once every ten days to go to the guard commandant's office; grand provisioner sent supplies, ordered them together face-to-face exhaust joy.
translations.idiomatic: Delighted with both brothers, the emperor ordered Zhao Li to visit the guard commandant every ten days while the imperial kitchen furnished banquets so they could feast together.

sentences.zh: 數年，禮卒，帝令孝從官屬送喪歸葬。

translations.literal: After several years Li died; the emperor ordered Xiao with official subordinates to escort the funeral home for burial.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhao Li died a few years later, the emperor had Zhao Xiao escort the coffin home with a full retinue.

sentences.zh: 後歲餘，復以□尉賜告歸，卒於家。

translations.literal: After more than a year, again as guard commandant granted leave to return; died at home.
translations.idiomatic: A year later Zhao Xiao retired from the guard command on sick leave and died at home.

sentences.zh: 孝無子，拜禮兩子為郎。

translations.literal: Xiao had no sons; appointed Li's two sons gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: Childless himself, he secured court appointments for Zhao Li's two sons.

sentences.zh: 時汝南有王琳巨尉者，年十餘歲喪父母。

translations.literal: At that time in Runan there was Wang Lin Juwei—age ten-something lost father and mother.
translations.idiomatic: About this time Wang Lin (style Juwei) of Runan lost both parents while still a boy.

sentences.zh: 因遭大亂，百姓奔逃，唯琳兄弟獨守塚廬，號泣不絕。

translations.literal: Because encountered great chaos, hundred surnames fled—only Lin's brothers alone guarded tomb hut, wailing without cease.
translations.idiomatic: When rebellion scattered the population, Wang Lin and his brothers alone stayed to mourn at the graves.

sentences.zh: 弟季，出遇赤眉，將為所哺，[一]琳自縛，請先季死，賊鄉而放遣，由是顯名鄉邑。

translations.literal: Younger brother Ji, going out met Red Eyebrows—was about to be eaten by them; [Note 1] Lin bound himself, begged to die before Ji; bandits faced him and released—thereby displayed name in village and district.
translations.idiomatic: When the Red Eyebrows seized his brother Ji for the pot, Wang Lin tied himself and begged to die first; moved, they freed both, and his fame spread locally.

sentences.zh: 後辟司徒府，薦士而退。

translations.literal: Later summoned to minister of education's office; recommended gentlemen and withdrew.
translations.idiomatic: Later he served the minister of education, recommended talented men, and retired.

sentences.zh: 注[一]哺，食之也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Bu means eat him.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Bu here means "to eat (someone)."

sentences.zh: 哺音補胡反。

translations.literal: Bu is read bu hu fan.
translations.idiomatic: Reading note for bu follows the commentary.

sentences.zh: 琅邪魏譚少閒者，時亦為饑寇所獲，等輩數十人皆束縛，以次當亨。

translations.literal: Wei Tan Shaoxian of Langya—at that time also was seized by famine bandits; several tens of peers were all bound—in order should be boiled.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Tan of Langya was captured with dozens of others by famine bandits lined up for the cookpot.

sentences.zh: 賊見譚似謹厚，獨令主□，暮輒執縛。

translations.literal: The bandits saw Tan seemed careful and thick—alone ordered him to oversee the cooking; each dusk then seized and bound.
translations.idiomatic: Thinking him trustworthy, they put him in charge of the kitchen but tied him up each night.

sentences.zh: 賊有夷長公，[一]特哀念譚，密解其縛，語曰：「汝曹皆應就食，急從此去。

translations.literal: Among the bandits was Yi Zhangong; [Note 1] specially pitied and thought of Tan, secretly untied his bonds, said to him: "You all ought to go to be eaten—hurry from here go.
translations.idiomatic: A captor named Yi Changong pitied Tan, freed him secretly, and whispered, "You will all be eaten—run now."

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「譚為諸君□，恆得遺余，餘人皆茹草萊，不如食我。

translations.literal: " He replied: "Tan for you gentlemen tends the cooking—always gets leftovers for me; the rest all chew weeds and greens—not as good as eat me.
translations.idiomatic: " Wei Tan replied, "I cook for you and keep scraps; the others eat only weeds—take me instead."

sentences.zh: 」長公義之，相曉赦遣，並得俱免。

translations.literal: " Changong righteous'd him, mutually informed and pardon released—together obtained both exemption.
translations.idiomatic: " Yi persuaded the gang to release everyone.

sentences.zh: 譚永平中為主家令。

translations.literal: Tan in Yongping became steward of the household.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yongping Wei Tan became steward of the imperial household.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]夷，姓也。

translations.literal: [Note 2] Comment [1] Yi is a surname.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 2] Note 1: Yi is the man's surname.

sentences.zh: 注[二]公主家令也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Princess household steward.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The office was steward of the princess establishment.

sentences.zh: 又齊國兒萌子明、[一]梁郡車成子威二人，兄弟並見執於赤眉，將食之，萌、成叩頭，乞以身代，賊亦哀而兩釋焉。

translations.literal: Moreover Qi kingdom's Er Meng Ziming, [Note 1] Liang commandery's Che Cheng Ziwei—two men; brothers together were seized by Red Eyebrows; were about to eat them; Meng, Cheng knocked heads, begged to substitute their bodies; bandits also pitied and released both.
translations.idiomatic: Er Meng of Qi and Che Cheng of Liang were seized with their brothers by the Red Eyebrows; both pairs offered their lives and were spared.

sentences.zh: 注[一]兒音五兮反。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Er is read wu xi fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Reading note for the surname Er follows.

sentences.zh: 淳于恭

translations.literal: Chunyu Gong.
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Gong.

sentences.zh: 淳於恭字孟孫，北海淳於人也。

translations.literal: Chunyu Gong, courtesy name Mengsun, was a man of Chunyu in Beihai.
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Gong, styled Mengsun, came from Chunyu in Beihai commandery.

sentences.zh: [一]善說老子，清靜不慕榮名。

translations.literal: [Note 1] Skilled at expounding the Laozi; clear and still; did not covet glory and fame.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] He lectured on the *Laozi*, lived quietly, and spurned renown.

sentences.zh: 家有山田果樹，人或侵盜，輒助為收采。

translations.literal: The household had mountain fields and fruit trees; if people encroached or stole, he always helped them gather the crop.
translations.idiomatic: He owned hillside orchards; when neighbors poached his fruit he helped them pick it.

sentences.zh: 又見偷刈禾者，恭念其愧，因伏草中，盜去乃起，裡落化之。

translations.literal: Again seeing one who stole cutting grain, Gong thought of his shame; therefore lay in the grass until the thief had gone and then rose—the village was transformed by him.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing someone steal his grain, he hid in the weeds so the thief would not be shamed; his hamlet took the lesson.

sentences.zh: 注[一]淳於，縣，故城*

translations.literal: Comment [1] Chunyu is a county; the old walled town site
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Chunyu county; the old citadel

translations.literal: (now)
translations.idiomatic: today

sentences.zh: *在*[今]*密州安丘縣東北，故淳於國也。

translations.literal: lies northeast of Anqiu county in Mizhou; it was the ancient state of Chunyu.
translations.idiomatic: stood northeast of modern Anqiu in Mizhou, on the ground of the old state of Chunyu.

sentences.zh: 王莽末，歲饑兵起，恭兄崇將為盜所亨，恭請代，得俱免。

translations.literal: At the end of Wang Mang, famine year, troops rose; Gong's elder brother Chong was about to be boiled by bandits; Gong begged to substitute—both obtained release.
translations.idiomatic: Late in Wang Mang's reign famine drove men to arms; when bandits meant to cook his brother Chong, Chunyu Gong offered himself instead and both lived.

sentences.zh: 後崇卒，恭養孤幼，教誨學問，有不如法，輒反用杖自棰，以感悟之，兒籩而改過。

translations.literal: After Chong died, Gong reared the orphaned young, taught and instructed learning; if there was not accordance with rule, always turned and used the staff to beat himself, to move them by feeling—the children felt shame and changed fault.
translations.idiomatic: After Chong died he raised the orphans and tutored them; when they erred he beat himself with a stick until they reformed.

sentences.zh: 初遭賊寇，百姓莫事農桑。

translations.literal: When first stricken by bandits, the hundred surnames did none tend farming and sericulture.
translations.idiomatic: After the raids nobody farmed or tended silkworms.

sentences.zh: 恭常獨力田耕，鄉人止之曰：「時方淆亂，死生未分，何空自苦為？」

translations.literal: Gong often alone exerted strength in field plowing; countrymen stopped him saying: "The time is just confused—life and death not yet divided—why vainly self-toil?"
translations.idiomatic: He kept farming alone; neighbors said, "Times are cruel and survival uncertain—why labor for nothing?"

sentences.zh: 恭曰：「縱我不得，它人何傷。

translations.literal: Gong said: "Though I obtain nothing, what harm to others?
translations.idiomatic: Gong replied, "If I gain nothing, no one else is hurt."

sentences.zh: 」墾耨不輟。

translations.literal: " He opened land and weeded without ceasing.
translations.idiomatic: " He never stopped working the soil.

sentences.zh: 後州郡連召，不應，遂幽居養志，潛於山澤。

translations.literal: Later provinces and commanderies summoned in succession—he did not respond; thereupon dwelt in seclusion nurturing intent, hid in mountains and marshes.
translations.idiomatic: Repeated summons went unanswered; he lived in seclusion among hills and marshes.

sentences.zh: 舉動周旋，必由禮度。

translations.literal: In raising acts and circling turns, always followed ritual measure.
translations.idiomatic: Every gesture observed ritual propriety.

sentences.zh: 建武中，郡舉孝廉，司空辟，皆不應，客隱琅邪黔陬山，遂數十年。

translations.literal: In Jianwu, commandery recommended Filially Pious and Incorrupt; minister of works summoned—all did not respond; as guest hid on Qianzou mountain in Langya—thus several tens of years.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Guangwu he declined nomination and ministerial appointment, hiding for decades on Mount Qianzou in Langya.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]黔陬縣之山也。

translations.literal: [Note 1] Comment [1] It is the mountain of Qianzou county.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] The hill lay in Qianzou county.

sentences.zh: 黔陬故城在今密州諸城縣東北也。

translations.literal: Qianzou's old town is northeast of Zhucheng county in Mizhou today.
translations.idiomatic: The old seat of Qianzou stood northeast of Zhucheng in Mizhou.

sentences.zh: 詔書□歎，賜谷千斛，刻石表閭。

translations.literal: Edicts praised him with admiration, granted a thousand hu of grain, carved stone to mark his gate.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial edicts sang his praise, sent a thousand hu of grain, and ordered an inscribed stone at his gate.

sentences.zh: 除子孝為太子舍人。

translations.literal: Appointed son Xiao as attendant of the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: His son Chunyu Xiao became an attendant of the crown prince.

sentences.zh: 江革字次翁，齊國臨淄人也。

translations.literal: Jiang Ge, courtesy name Ciweng, was a man of Linzi in the Qi kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: Jiang Ge, styled Ciweng, was a native of Linzi in Qi.

sentences.zh: 少失父，獨與母居。

translations.literal: In youth lost his father; alone dwelt with his mother.
translations.idiomatic: His father died when he was young, leaving only him and his mother.

sentences.zh: 遭天下亂，盜賊並起，革負母逃難，備經阻險，常採拾以為養。

translations.literal: Encountered realm in chaos, bandits rising together; Ge bore his mother fleeing hardship, fully experienced perilous obstacles; often gathered and picked to provide nourishment.
translations.idiomatic: When rebellion erupted he carried his mother through danger and foraged to feed her.

sentences.zh: 數遇賊，或劫欲將去，革輒涕泣求哀，言有老母，辭氣願款，有足感動人者。

translations.literal: Several times met bandits; sometimes seized and wished to carry him off—Ge always wept tears begging pity, spoke of having old mother; phrase and breath earnest and sincere—there were enough to move people.
translations.idiomatic: Robbers often seized him; he wept that he must care for his aged mother, and his earnest plea moved them.

sentences.zh: [一]賊以是不忍犯之，或乃指避兵之方，[二] 遂得俱全於難。

translations.literal: [Note 1] Bandits therefore did not bear to violate him; sometimes even pointed to methods of avoiding troops [Note 2] —thus was able wholly preserved in hardship.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] They spared him; some even showed him escape routes. [Note 2] He and his mother survived.

sentences.zh: 革轉客下邳，窮貧裸跣，行傭以供母，便身之物，莫不必給。

translations.literal: Ge shifted as guest to Xiapi; utterly poor bare-footed; performed hired labor to supply his mother; convenient-for-body things—none not necessarily supplied.
translations.idiomatic: As a refugee in Xiapi he went barefoot and hired out his labor, giving his mother every comfort he could.

sentences.zh: 注[一]願，謹也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Yuan means careful.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Yuan here means "careful" or "respectful."

sentences.zh: 款，誠也。

translations.literal: Kuan means sincere.
translations.idiomatic: Kuan means sincere.

sentences.zh: 注[二]華嶠書曰「語以避兵道」也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Hua Qiao's book says "told him the way to avoid troops."
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Hua Qiao records that someone showed him a safe route.

sentences.zh: 建武末年，與母歸鄉里。

translations.literal: At the end of Jianwu, with his mother returned to village.
translations.idiomatic: Late in the Jianwu era he brought his mother home.

sentences.zh: 每至歲時，縣當案比，[一]革以母老，不欲搖動，自在轅中挽車，不用牛馬，由是鄉里稱之曰「江巨孝」。

translations.literal: Whenever at year's seasons the county ought inspect households [Note 1]—Ge because mother old did not wish to shake her; himself in the axle-tree dragged the cart, used no ox or horse—thereby village and district called him "Jiang the Great Filial."
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] At the annual household census he pulled the cart himself so his mother would not jolt—earning the nickname "Great Filial Jiang."

sentences.zh: [二]太守嘗備禮召，革以母老不應。

translations.literal: [Note 2] The grand administrator once with full ritual summoned; Ge because mother old did not respond.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 2] The prefect summoned him with ceremony; he declined while his mother lived.

sentences.zh: 及母終，至性殆滅，嘗寢伏頤廬，服竟，不忍除。

translations.literal: When mother ended, utmost nature nearly extinguished; once slept prone at forehead hut; when mourning ended, could not bear to remove.
translations.idiomatic: Her death shattered him; he lay beside the tomb shelter and refused to shed mourning garb when the term ended.

sentences.zh: 郡守遣丞掾釋服，因請以為吏。

translations.literal: The grand administrator sent assistant and clerk to release mourning; thereby requested to make him clerk.
translations.idiomatic: The prefect sent aides to end his mourning by fiat and offered him office.

sentences.zh: 注[一]案驗以比之，猶今魍閱也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Inspect and verify to compare—like today's household review.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The census inspection matched the later household review.

sentences.zh: 注[二]巨，大也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Ju means great.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Ju means "great."

sentences.zh: 華嶠書曰「臨淄令楊音高之，設特席，顯異巨孝於稠人廣觿中，親奉錢以助供養」也。

translations.literal: Hua Qiao's book says "Magistrate of Linzi Yang Yin esteemed him, set a special mat, displayed differently Great Filial amid dense people wide youths—personally presented cash to aid provision."
translations.idiomatic: Hua Qiao adds that Magistrate Yang Yin of Linzi honored him before a crowd with gifts of cash.

sentences.zh: 永平初，舉孝廉為郎，補楚太僕。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Yongping, recommended Filially Pious and Incorrupt as gentleman; supplemented as grand coachman of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Yongping he was nominated filially pious and incorrupt, named gentleman, and assigned grand coachman of Chu.

sentences.zh: 月餘，自劾去。

translations.literal: After more than a month, impeached himself and left.
translations.idiomatic: Within a month he resigned.

sentences.zh: 楚王英馳遣官屬追之，遂不肯還。

translations.literal: Prince Ying of Chu urgently sent officials to pursue him—finally would not return.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Liu Ying of Chu sent riders after him, but he would not come back.

sentences.zh: 復使中傅贈送，辭不受。

translations.literal: Again ordered palace tutor to present gifts—declined and did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: The prince's tutor offered gifts; he refused.

sentences.zh: 後數應三公命，輒去。

translations.literal: Later several times answered Three Excellencies' orders—always left.
translations.idiomatic: Later summons from the Three Excellencies never held him.

sentences.zh: 建初初，太尉牟融舉賢良方正，再遷司空長史。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Jianchu, Grand Commandant Mou Rong recommended worthy and upright; twice promoted minister of works chief clerk.
translations.idiomatic: At the start of Jianchu Mou Rong nominated him worthy and upright; he rose to chief clerk of the minister of works.

sentences.zh: 肅宗甚崇禮之，遷五官中郎將。

translations.literal: Suzong greatly revered and ritual'd him; promoted general of the household for all five offices.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhang honored him and promoted him to general of the household for all five offices.

sentences.zh: 每朝會，帝常使虎賁扶侍，及進拜，恆目禮焉。

translations.literal: At each court assembly the emperor always ordered tiger-guards to support and attend; when advancing to bow, constantly eyed him with ritual.
translations.idiomatic: At audience the emperor had guards steady him and watched him bow with special respect.

sentences.zh: [一]時有疾不會，輒太官送醪膳，恩寵有殊。

translations.literal: [Note 1] When ill did not attend—always grand provisioner sent ale and viands; favor and grace had distinction.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] When illness kept him away, the imperial kitchen sent wine and food—a singular honor.

sentences.zh: 於是京師貴戚□尉馬廖、侍中竇憲慕其行，各奉書致禮，革無所報受。

translations.literal: Thereupon capital noble kin Captain Ma Liao of the guard corps, attendant Dou Xian admired his conduct; each presented letters and gifts—Ge had nothing to report or accept.
translations.idiomatic: Captain Ma Liao and attendant Dou Xian courted him with letters and gifts; he answered none.

sentences.zh: [二]帝聞而益善之。

translations.literal: [Note 2] The emperor heard and increasingly approved him.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 2] The emperor admired him all the more.

sentences.zh: 後上書乞骸骨，轉拜諫議大夫，賜告歸，因謝病稱篤。

translations.literal: Later submitted memorial begging old bones; transferred appointed grandee remonstrant; granted leave to return; thereupon declined illness claiming grave.
translations.idiomatic: He asked to retire, received appointment as grandee remonstrant with leave to go home, then pleaded grave illness.

sentences.zh: 注[一]獨視之也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Alone looked at him.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The emperor singled him out with his gaze.

sentences.zh: 注[二]華嶠書曰：「終不報書，一無所受。」

translations.literal: Comment [2] Hua Qiao's book says: "Finally never answered letters—nothing accepted."
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Hua Qiao says he never replied and accepted nothing.

sentences.zh: 元和中，天子思革至行，制詔齊相曰：「諫議大夫江革，前以病歸，今起居何如？

translations.literal: In Yuanhe, the Son of Heaven thought of Ge's utmost conduct; regulated edict to Qi chancellor saying: "Grandee Remonstrant Jiang Ge, formerly because of illness returned—how are his rising and dwelling now?
translations.idiomatic: During Yuanhe the emperor remembered Jiang Ge's devotion and asked the Qi chancellor how his health was since retiring ill.

sentences.zh: 夫孝，百行之冠，觿善之始也。

translations.literal: Now filial piety is the crown of hundred conducts; the beginning of good among youths.
translations.idiomatic: Filial duty crowns every virtue and is the root of goodness.

sentences.zh: 國家每惟志士，未嘗不及革。

translations.literal: The state whenever thinks on devoted gentlemen—never fails to reach Ge.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the court honors worthy men, Jiang Ge comes to mind.

sentences.zh: 縣以見谷千斛賜『巨孝』，常以八月長吏存問，致羊酒，以終厥身。

translations.literal: Let the county with present grain thousand hu grant 'Great Filial'; regularly in eighth month senior clerk inquire after him; send sheep and wine—to end his body.
translations.idiomatic: Award him a thousand hu from county stores as Great Filial; each eighth month send officials with sheep and wine for life.

sentences.zh: [一]如有不幸，祠以中牢。

translations.literal: [Note 1] If there be misfortune—sacrifice with middle victim.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] At his death sacrifice with the middle victim offering.

sentences.zh: 」由是「巨孝」之稱，行於天下。

translations.literal: " Thereupon the title "Great Filial"—walked under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: " The title Great Filial Jiang spread across the realm.

sentences.zh: 及卒，詔復賜谷千斛。

translations.literal: When he died, edict again granted grain thousand hu.
translations.idiomatic: At his death another thousand hu was granted.

sentences.zh: 注[一]華嶠書曰：「致羊一頭，酒二斛。」

translations.literal: Comment [1] Hua Qiao's book says: "Deliver one sheep head, two hu wine."
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Hua Qiao specifies one sheep and two hu of wine.

sentences.zh: 劉般字伯興，宣帝之玄孫也。

translations.literal: Liu Ban, courtesy name Boxing, was Emperor Xuan's great-great-grandson.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ban, styled Boxing, was great-great-grandson of Emperor Xuan.

sentences.zh: 宣帝封子囂於楚，是為孝王。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan enfeoffed son Xiao in Chu—this is King Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan enfeoffed his son Liu Xiao in Chu as King Xiao.

sentences.zh: 孝王生思王衍，衍生王紆，紆生般。

translations.literal: King Xiao begat reflective King Yan; Yan begat King Yu; Yu begat Ban.
translations.idiomatic: The line ran King Xiao to King Yan to King Yu to Liu Ban.

sentences.zh: 自囂至般，積累仁義，世有名節，而紆尤慈篤。

translations.literal: From Xiao to Ban, accumulated benevolence and righteousness; generations had famed integrity—but Yu especially kind and earnest.
translations.idiomatic: The house accumulated renown for benevolence; Liu Yu was especially devoted.

sentences.zh: 早失母，同產弟原鄉侯平尚幼，紆親自鞠養，常與共臥起飲食。

translations.literal: Early lost mother; younger brother of same birth Marquis of Yuanxiang Ping still young—Yu personally nursed and reared; always shared lying rising drinking eating.
translations.idiomatic: After his mother died he raised his infant brother Ping himself, eating and sleeping beside him.

sentences.zh: 及成人，未嘗離左右。

translations.literal: When grown to man, never left left-right.
translations.idiomatic: As adults they were never apart.

sentences.zh: 平病卒，紆哭泣歐血，數月亦歿。

translations.literal: Ping sick died; Yu wept vomiting blood—several months also died.
translations.idiomatic: When Ping died Liu Yu coughed blood with grief and followed him within months.

sentences.zh: 初，紆襲王封，因值王莽篡位，廢為庶人，因家於彭城。

translations.literal: At first Yu inherited king's enfeoffment; because encountered Wang Mang's usurpation—abolished as commoner; therefore made home at Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Yu lost his title when Wang Mang seized the throne and settled as a commoner in Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 般數歲而孤，獨與母居。

translations.literal: Ban several years old then orphaned; alone dwelt with mother.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ban was orphaned young and lived only with his mother.

sentences.zh: 王莽敗，天下亂，太夫人聞更始即位，[一]乃將般俱奔長安。

translations.literal: Wang Mang defeated, realm in chaos; Grand Lady heard Gengshi ascended [Note 1]—thereupon led Ban together fleeing to Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] After Wang Mang fell she fled with Liu Ban to Chang'an when she heard the Gengshi Emperor had ascended.

sentences.zh: 會更始敗，復與般轉側兵革中，西行上隴，遂流至武威。

translations.literal: When Gengshi failed, again with Ban tossed in weapons and armor; west proceeded ascended Long—finally drifted to Wuwei.
translations.idiomatic: When that regime collapsed they wandered west through the Long corridor to Wuwei.

sentences.zh: 般雖尚少，而篤志修行，講誦不怠。

translations.literal: Ban although still young—yet earnest intent cultivated conduct; lectured and recited without slack.
translations.idiomatic: Though young Liu Ban studied relentlessly.

sentences.zh: 其母及諸舅，以為身寄絕域，死生未必，[二]不宜苦精若此，數以曉般，般猶不改其業。

translations.literal: His mother and various maternal uncles thought body lodged in extreme frontier—life and death not yet settled [Note 2]—not fitting to toil essence thus; several times used to dawn Ban—Ban still did not change his pursuit.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 2] Kin urged him to ease his studies in the frontier wilds; he refused.

sentences.zh: 注[一]太夫人，般之母也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Grand Lady is Ban's mother.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Grand Lady denotes Liu Ban's mother.

sentences.zh: 前書音義曰：「列侯之妻稱夫人，母稱太夫人。」

translations.literal: Former book sounds and meanings says: "Marquis's wife is called lady; mother called grand lady."
translations.idiomatic: The Han glossary states that a marquis's wife is lady and his mother grand lady.

sentences.zh: 注[二]「必」或作「分」也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] "Certain" sometimes written "divide."
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Some texts read "uncertain" instead of "not yet certain."

sentences.zh: 建武八年，隗囂敗，河西始通，般即將家屬東至洛陽，修經學於師門。

translations.literal: Jianwu eighth year, Wei Xiao defeated—Hexi first opened; Ban then led household east to Luoyang, cultivated classics learning at master's gate.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 8 the Hexi corridor opened and Liu Ban moved his family to Luoyang to study the classics.

sentences.zh: 明年，光武下詔，封般為菑丘侯，奉孝王祀，使就國。

translations.literal: Next year Guangwu issued edict—enfeoffed Ban as marquis of Zaiqiu, observing King Xiao's sacrifices; ordered him go to state.
translations.idiomatic: The next year Emperor Guangwu named him marquis of Zaiqiu to maintain the cult of King Xiao.

sentences.zh: 後以國屬楚王，徙封杼秋侯。

translations.literal: Later because state belonged to Chu prince—transferred enfeoffment marquis of Zhuqiu.
translations.idiomatic: When his territory passed to the prince of Chu he became marquis of Zhuqiu.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]杼秋，縣，屬梁國。

translations.literal: [Note 1] Comment [1] Zhuqiu is a county belonging to Liang kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] Zhuqiu county lay in the kingdom of Liang.

sentences.zh: 杼音是與反。

translations.literal: Zhu is read shi yu fan.
translations.idiomatic: Reading note for zhu follows.

sentences.zh: 十九年，行幸沛，詔問郡中諸侯行能。

translations.literal: Nineteenth year, traveled favorably to Pei—edict asked commandery's various marquises conduct and ability.
translations.idiomatic: In year 19 the emperor toured Pei and asked after each marquis.

sentences.zh: 太守薦言般束修至行，為諸侯師。

translations.literal: Grand administrator recommended saying Ban bundle-repair utmost conduct—was marquises' teacher.
translations.idiomatic: The prefect reported Liu Ban's disciplined virtue as a model for the other nobles.

sentences.zh: [一] 帝聞而嘉之，乃賜般綬，錢百萬，繒二百匹。

translations.literal: [Note 1] The emperor heard and praised him—thereupon granted Ban ribbon, cash million, silk two hundred bolts.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] The emperor rewarded him with ribbons, a million cash, and two hundred bolts of silk.

sentences.zh: 二十年，復與車駕會沛，因從還洛陽，賜谷什物，留為侍祠侯。

translations.literal: Twentieth year, again with imperial carriage met at Pei; thereupon followed returned Luoyang—granted grain assorted goods; retained as attendant-sacrifice marquis.
translations.idiomatic: In year 20 he joined the emperor at Pei, returned to Luoyang, received grain and goods, and stayed as marquis attendant on sacrifice.

sentences.zh: 注[一]束修謂謹束修絜也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Bundle-repair means careful bundle repair purity.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The phrase means disciplined self-cultivation.

sentences.zh: 永平元年，以國屬沛，徙封居巢侯，[一]復隨諸侯就國。

translations.literal: Yongping first year, because state belonged to Pei—transferred enfeoffment marquis of Ju Chao [Note 1]—again followed various marquises go to states.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 1 his fief attached to Pei and he became marquis of Ju Chao, joining other nobles in taking up their states.

sentences.zh: 數年，楊州刺史觀恂薦般在國口無擇言，行無怨惡，宜蒙旌顯。

translations.literal: After several years, Yangzhou governor Guan Xun recommended Ban in state mouth had no selective speech—conduct had no resentful evil—ought receive banner display.
translations.idiomatic: Years later Inspector Guan Xun of Yangzhou praised his blameless speech and conduct.

sentences.zh: 顯宗嘉之。

translations.literal: Xianzong praised him.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ming approved.

sentences.zh: 十年，征般行執金吾事，從至南陽，還為朝侯。

translations.literal: Tenth year, summoned Ban to act bearer of gilded mace affairs; followed to Nanyang—returned as court marquis.
translations.idiomatic: In year 10 he served as acting bearer of the gilded mace on campaign to Nanyang, then returned as marquis at court.

sentences.zh: 明年，兼屯騎校尉。

translations.literal: Next year, concurrently colonel of garrison cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: The next year he added colonel of garrison cavalry.

sentences.zh: 時五校官顯職閒，而府寺寬敞，輿服光麗，伎巧畢給，故多以宗室肺腑居之。

translations.literal: At that time five colonels' offices were prominent posts leisurely; but offices and temples broad and spacious—carriages and robes bright and lovely—craft and skill fully supplied—therefore much used imperial-house lung-and-loin to dwell in them.
translations.idiomatic: Those cushioned commands went mostly to imperial kinsmen because of their leisure and splendor.

sentences.zh: [二]每行幸郡國，般常將長水胡騎從。

translations.literal: [Note 2] Whenever traveled favorably commanderies and states—Ban always led Changshui Hu horsemen followed.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 2] On imperial tours he commanded the Changshui Hu cavalry escort.

sentences.zh: 注[一]居巢，縣，屬廬江郡也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Ju Chao is a county belonging to Lujiang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Ju Chao county lay in Lujiang commandery.

sentences.zh: 注[二]肺腑，天子之親屬也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Lung-and-loin—Son of Heaven's kin.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Lung-and-loin means close imperial kin.

sentences.zh: 帝曾欲置常平倉，[一]公卿議者多以為便。

translations.literal: The emperor once wished to establish ever-normal granaries [Note 1]—dukes and ministers who deliberated mostly thought convenient.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] Emperor Ming once proposed ever-normal granaries; most ministers approved.

sentences.zh: 般對以「常平倉外有利民之名，而內實侵刻百姓，豪右因緣為奸，小民不能得其平，置之不便」。

translations.literal: Ban replied with "Ever-normal granaries outward have name of benefiting people—but inward truly encroach and carve the hundred surnames; magnates seize occasion for villainy—small people cannot obtain their fairness—establishing them not convenient."
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ban argued that granaries benefited magnates while harming commoners and should not be built.

sentences.zh: 帝乃止。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon stopped.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor dropped the plan.

sentences.zh: 是時下令禁民二業，[二]又以郡國牛疫，通使區種增耕，[三]而吏下檢結，多失其實，百姓患之。

translations.literal: At this time issued order forbidding people's second occupations [Note 2]; moreover because commanderies and kingdoms ox epidemic—sent orders regional planting increase plowing [Note 3]—but clerks below inspect tie—mostly missed reality—hundred surnames suffered it.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 2] A ban on sideline trades coincided with cattle plague and pit-farming orders. [Note 3] Official inspections missed the mark and angered the people.

sentences.zh: 般上言：「郡國以官禁二業，至有田者不得漁捕。

translations.literal: Ban submitted upward: "Commanderies and kingdoms because officials forbid second occupations—even those who have fields may not fish and net.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ban wrote that the sideline ban barred landowners from fishing.

sentences.zh: 今濱江湖郡率少蠶桑，民資漁采以助口實，且以冬春閒月，不妨農事。

translations.literal: Now shore river-lake commanderies generally lack silkworm mulberry—people rely on fishing gathering to aid mouth substance; moreover using winter spring idle months—does not obstruct farming affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Lake counties lack silk and rely on fishing in winter months without harming the harvest.

sentences.zh: 夫漁獵之利，為田除害，有助谷食，無關二業也。

translations.literal: Now hunting-fishing profit—for fields removes harm—helps grain food—not related to second occupations.
translations.idiomatic: Hunting and fishing clear pests and supplement grain—they are not illicit sidelines.

sentences.zh: 又郡國以牛疫、水旱，墾田多減，故詔□區種，增進頃畝，以為民也。

translations.literal: Moreover, because of cattle plague and flood or drought in the commanderies and kingdoms, reclaimed fields were greatly reduced; therefore an edict ordered cluster-field planting and advanced the acreage under cultivation, for the sake of the people.
