translations.idiomatic: Across the commanderies and kingdoms, cattle disease and bad weather had cut deeply into tillage, so the court ordered cluster-field methods and pushed to bring more acres under the plow for the people's sake.

sentences.zh: 而吏舉度田，欲令多前，[四]至於不種之處，亦通為租。

translations.literal: Yet when officials surveyed fields for assessment, they wished to make the figures exceed those of prior years, [Note 4] so that even places that were not sown were altogether charged rent.
translations.idiomatic: But officials measuring fields for the tax rolls inflated totals beyond earlier years, even assessing rent on land that had never been planted.

sentences.zh: 可申□刺史、二千石，務令實核，其有增加，皆使與奪田同罪。

translations.literal: It is fitting to instruct the inspectors-in-chief and officials of two thousand piculs, enjoining them to verify the facts; wherever there are increases, let all such cases be punished like wrongful seizure of fields.
translations.idiomatic: The inspectors-in-chief and two-thousand-picul governors must be told to check numbers against reality; padded assessments should be punished as severely as stealing peasants' land.

sentences.zh: 」帝悉從之。

translations.literal: " The Emperor approved all of it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted every point.

sentences.zh: [五] 注[一]宣帝時，大司農耿壽昌請令邊郡皆築倉，以谷賤時增其價而糴之以利農，谷貴時減價而糶之，名曰常平倉。

translations.literal: [Note 5] Note [1]: Under Emperor Xuan, the Minister of Agriculture Geng Shouchang requested that every frontier commandery build granaries; when grain was cheap they would raise the price and buy it in to benefit farmers, and when grain was dear they would lower the price and sell it out—these were called the Ever-Normal Granaries.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 5] Comment [1]: Under Emperor Xuan, Minister of Agriculture Geng Shouchang had frontier counties build granaries that bought cheap grain to help farmers and sold when prices spiked—the "Ever-Normal" stores.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謂農者不得商賈也。

translations.literal: Note [2]: This means farmers were not allowed to engage in trade.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Farmers were barred from acting as merchants.

sentences.zh: 注[三]泛勝之書曰：「上農區田*

translations.literal: Note [3]: The Book of Fan Shengzhi says: "For top-grade farmers, cluster fields
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Fan Shengzhi writes that for the best farmers, cluster-field plots

translations.literal: (large)
translations.idiomatic: the large method:

sentences.zh: **[法]*，區方深各六寸，閒相去七寸，一畝三千七百區，丁男女種十畝，至秋收區三升粟，畝得百斛。

translations.literal: **[method]*: each plot's length, width, and depth are six cun; the spacing between them is seven cun; one mu holds three thousand seven hundred plots; adult men and women plant ten mu; at autumn harvest each plot yields three sheng of millet, and one mu yields one hundred hu.
translations.idiomatic: Each pit is six cun square and deep, seven cun apart; one mu takes 3,700 pits; a couple works ten mu; at harvest each pit yields three sheng of millet, about one hundred hu per mu.

sentences.zh: 中農區田法，方七寸，深六寸，閒相去二尺，一畝千二十七區，丁男女種十畝，秋收粟畝得五十一石。

translations.literal: The cluster-field method for middling farmers: seven cun square, six cun deep, spacing two chi apart; one mu has one thousand twenty-seven plots; adult men and women plant ten mu; at autumn harvest millet yield per mu is fifty-one shi.
translations.idiomatic: For middling farmers the pits are seven cun square, six deep, two chi apart—1,027 per mu, ten mu per couple—with yields of fifty-one shi of millet per mu.

sentences.zh: 下農區田法，方九寸，深六寸，閒相去三尺，秋收畝得二十八石。

translations.literal: The cluster-field method for low-grade farmers: nine cun square, six cun deep, spacing three chi apart; at autumn harvest yield per mu is twenty-eight shi.
translations.idiomatic: The lowest tier uses nine-cun pits, six cun deep, three chi apart, yielding twenty-eight shi per mu.

sentences.zh: 旱即以水沃之。」

translations.literal: If it is dry, water them at once."
translations.idiomatic: In drought, irrigate the pits without delay."

sentences.zh: 注[四]多於前歲。

translations.literal: Note [4]: More than the previous year.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: Meaning higher figures than the year before.

sentences.zh: 注[五]華嶠書*

translations.literal: Note [5]: Hua Qiao's book
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: In Hua Qiao's history

translations.literal: (says)
translations.idiomatic: reads

sentences.zh: *「奪」作「脫」也。

translations.literal: * "seize" is written "release."
translations.idiomatic: uses "release" where this text has "seize."

sentences.zh: 肅宗即位，以為長樂少府。

translations.literal: When Emperor Suzong ascended the throne, he appointed him Superintendent of the Palace of Eternal Joy.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhang installed him as superintendent of the Changle palace workshops.

sentences.zh: 建初二年，遷宗正。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianchu, he was promoted to Director of the Imperial Clan.
translations.idiomatic: In 77 CE he became director of the imperial clan office.

sentences.zh: 般妻卒，厚加賵贈，及賜頤塋地於顯節陵下。

translations.literal: When Ban's wife died, generous funeral gifts were conferred, and burial ground at the foot of the Mausoleum of Manifest Virtue was granted.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Ban's wife died, the court sent lavish funeral gifts and land for her tomb below Emperor Guangwu's mausoleum.

sentences.zh: 般在位數言政事。

translations.literal: While in office Ban repeatedly spoke on government affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ban often spoke his mind on policy while serving.

sentences.zh: 其收恤九族，行義尤著，時人稱之。

translations.literal: In gathering and relieving his nine branches of kin, his conduct of righteousness was especially notable, and people of the time praised him.
translations.idiomatic: His care for every branch of the clan and his upright conduct won wide admiration.

sentences.zh: 年六十，建初三年卒。

translations.literal: At age sixty, he died in the third year of Jianchu.
translations.idiomatic: He died at sixty in 78 CE.

sentences.zh: 子憲嗣。

translations.literal: His son Xian succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Xian inherited the title.

sentences.zh: 憲卒，子重嗣。

translations.literal: When Xian died, his son Chong succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Xian died, Liu Chong succeeded.

sentences.zh: 憲兄愷。

translations.literal: Xian's elder brother Kai.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xian's elder brother was Liu Kai.

sentences.zh: 愷字伯豫，以當襲般爵，讓與弟憲，遁逃避封。

translations.literal: Kai, courtesy name Boyu, since he was due to inherit Ban's noble rank, yielded it to his younger brother Xian and fled into hiding to avoid investiture.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Kai (Boyu) was heir to Liu Ban's marquisate but gave it to his younger brother Liu Xian and went into hiding to refuse the seal.

sentences.zh: 久之，章和中，有司奏請絕愷國，肅宗美其義，特優假之，[一]愷猶不出。

translations.literal: After a long time, during the Zhanghe era, the relevant offices memorialized to abolish Kai's state; Emperor Suzong admired his righteousness and specially granted him indulgent delay, [Note 1] yet Kai still did not emerge.
translations.idiomatic: Years later, in the Zhanghe period (87–89), officials moved to strip Liu Kai's fief; Emperor Zhang admired his integrity and gave him extra time, but Liu Kai still refused to come out.

sentences.zh: 積十餘歲，至永元十年，有司復奏之，侍中賈逵因上書曰：「孔子稱『能以禮讓為國，於從政乎何有』。

translations.literal: More than ten years accumulated; by the tenth year of Yongyuan, the offices again memorialized on it; Palace Attendant Jia Kui therefore submitted a memorial saying: "Confucius praised 'He who can govern a state by ritual deference—what difficulty would he have in administering?'".
translations.idiomatic: After more than a decade, in 98 CE, officials tried again; Palace Attendant Jia Kui cited Confucius: "Rule with courtesy and yielding—what trouble could politics bring?".

sentences.zh: [二]竊見居巢侯劉般嗣子愷，素行孝友，謙遜絜清，讓封弟憲，潛身遠多。

translations.literal: [Note 2] I have observed that Kai, heir of the Marquis of Ju-chao Liu Ban, by nature practices filial piety and fraternal duty, is humble, modest, and pure, yielded his enfeoffment to his younger brother Xian, and concealed himself far away for long.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 2] Your servant sees Liu Kai, heir to Marquis Liu Ban of Ju-chao—filial, brotherly, humble, and scrupulous—who gave the title to Liu Xian and has lived in hiding for years.

sentences.zh: 有司不原樂善之心，而繩以循常之法，[三]懼非長克讓之風，成含弘之化。

translations.literal: The offices do not trace the mind that delights in goodness, but apply the rope of routine law, [Note 3] and I fear this will not lengthen the wind of yielding nor accomplish the transforming influence of magnanimity.
translations.idiomatic: Bureaucrats ignore his generous intent and apply rigid rules; [Note 3] that undercuts the habit of yielding and the court's ideal of broad-minded rule.

sentences.zh: 前世扶陽侯韋玄成，[四]近有陵陽侯丁鴻、鄳侯鄧彪，[五]並以高行絜身辭爵，未聞貶削，而皆登三事。

translations.literal: In a former age Marquis of Fuyang Wei Xuancheng, [Note 4] and recently Marquis of Lingyang Ding Hong and Marquis of Mo Deng Biao, [Note 5] all declined noble rank with lofty conduct and pure persons; it was not heard that they were degraded or cut down, and they all ascended to the Three Offices.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xuancheng once renounced his marquisate; more recently Ding Hong of Lingyang and Deng Biao of Mo did the same—none were punished for it, and all rose to the three highest posts.

sentences.zh: 今愷景仰前修，有伯夷之節，[六]宜蒙矜宥，全其先功，以增聖朝尚德之美。

translations.literal: Now Kai looks up to former exemplars and has the integrity of Boyi; [Note 6] he ought to receive pity and forgiveness, his ancestor's merit be kept whole, so as to increase the splendor of this sage court's esteem for virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Kai follows past models and matches Boyi's renunciation; [Note 6] he deserves mercy—preserve his family's merit and burnish the dynasty's reputation for honoring virtue.

sentences.zh: 」和帝納之，下詔曰：「故居巢侯劉般嗣子愷，當襲般爵，而稱父遺意，致國弟憲，遁亡七年，所守彌篤。

translations.literal: " Emperor He accepted it and issued an edict saying: "The former Marquis of Ju-chao Liu Ban's heir Kai was due to inherit Ban's rank, but citing his late father's intent he transferred the state to his younger brother Xian; he has fled and been absent seven years, yet his resolve grows ever firmer.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor He agreed and proclaimed: "Liu Kai, Liu Ban's heir, was to succeed the Ju-chao marquisate but, honoring his father's wishes, passed it to Liu Xian and vanished for seven years—his resolve only deepened."

sentences.zh: 蓋王法崇善，成人之美。

translations.literal: For the royal law exalts goodness and completes others' fine deeds.
translations.idiomatic: The law exists to reward virtue and help men finish what they begin.

sentences.zh: 其聽憲嗣爵。

translations.literal: Let Xian be permitted to succeed to the noble rank.
translations.idiomatic: Let Liu Xian hold the title.

sentences.zh: 遭事之宜，後不得以為比。

translations.literal: According to the fitness of the circumstance, later cases may not cite this as precedent.
translations.idiomatic: This is a one-time accommodation; do not treat it as precedent.

sentences.zh: 」乃征愷，拜為郎，稍遷侍中。

translations.literal: " Thereupon Kai was summoned, appointed gentleman, and gradually promoted to palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Kai was summoned, made a gentleman of the court, and rose to palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 注[一]假，借也。

translations.literal: Note [1]: Jia means lend.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Here "jia" means to grant on loan or extend forbearance.

sentences.zh: 注[二]論語之文也。

translations.literal: Note [2]: This is text from the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The quotation comes from the Analects.

sentences.zh: 何有者，言*

translations.literal: As for "what difficulty," it means saying
translations.idiomatic: The gloss explains "what difficulty" as

sentences.zh: (善無)

translations.literal: (for goodness there is no)
translations.idiomatic: "for those who practice goodness there is no"

sentences.zh: **[何難之]*有也。

translations.literal: **[what difficulty]* therein."
translations.idiomatic: real obstacle"—that is, none at all."

sentences.zh: 注[三]原，本也。

translations.literal: Note [3]: Yuan means root.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: "Yuan" here means to trace to the root.

sentences.zh: 繩，政也。

translations.literal: Sheng means apply standards.
translations.idiomatic: "Sheng" means to judge by fixed rule.

sentences.zh: 注[四]玄成字少翁，韋賢薨，讓封于兄弘。

translations.literal: Note [4]: Xuancheng's courtesy name was Shaoweng; when Wei Xian died, he yielded the enfeoffment to his elder brother Hong.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: Wei Xuancheng (Shaoweng) declined his father's marquisate in favor of his brother Wei Hong after Wei Xian died.

sentences.zh: 宣帝高其節，以為河南太守。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan esteemed his integrity and appointed him Governor of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan admired his integrity and named him governor of Henan.

sentences.zh: 元帝時為御史大夫，又為丞相。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Yuan he was imperial counselor, then chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Yuan he rose to imperial counselor and then chancellor.

sentences.zh: 見前書也。

translations.literal: See the former Han book.
translations.idiomatic: See the account in the Han shu.

sentences.zh: 注[五]鴻讓國於弟盛，和帝時為司徒。

translations.literal: Note [5]: Hong yielded the state to his younger brother Sheng; under Emperor He he was Minister of Education.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: Ding Hong gave his fief to his brother Ding Sheng and later served Emperor He as minister of education.

sentences.zh: 彪讓國於弟荊、鳳，明帝時為太尉。

translations.literal: Biao yielded the state to his younger brothers Jing and Feng; under Emperor Ming he was grand commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Biao passed his title to his brothers Deng Jing and Deng Feng and became grand commandant under Emperor Ming.

sentences.zh: 鄳音盲。

translations.literal: Mo is pronounced mang.
translations.idiomatic: The marquisate name Mo is read like the syllable "mang."

sentences.zh: 注[六]景猶慕也。

translations.literal: Note [6]: Jing means admire.
translations.idiomatic: Note [6]: Here "jing" carries the sense of "admire."

sentences.zh: 詩云：「景行行止。

translations.literal: The Book of Poetry says: "Broad that highway, along it we walk and rest."
translations.idiomatic: The Shijing says, "Lofty that great road—take it and find your place."

sentences.zh: 」前修，前賢也。

translations.literal: " Former cultivated ones means former worthies.
translations.idiomatic: "Former exemplars" means the worthies of earlier ages.

sentences.zh: 楚辭曰：「蹇吾法夫前修。」

translations.literal: The Songs of Chu says: "Alas, I take my model from former cultivated ones."
translations.idiomatic: The Chuci says, "I pattern myself after the ancients who walked this hard road."

sentences.zh: 愷之入朝，在位者莫不仰其風行。

translations.literal: When Kai entered court, those in office none failed to look up to the wind of his conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Once Liu Kai returned to court, every colleague looked up to his example.

sentences.zh: 遷步兵校尉。

translations.literal: He was promoted colonel of infantry.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted to colonel of infantry.

sentences.zh: 十三年，遷宗正，免。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year, he was promoted Director of the Imperial Clan, then dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: In the thirteenth year of Yongyuan (101 CE) he became director of the imperial clan, then was removed.

sentences.zh: 復拜侍中，遷長水校尉。

translations.literal: He was again appointed palace attendant and promoted colonel of Changshui.
translations.idiomatic: He was recalled as palace attendant and then named colonel of the Changshui regiment.

sentences.zh: 永初元年，代周章為太常。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongchu, he replaced Zhou Zhang as Minister of Ceremonies.
translations.idiomatic: In 107 CE he succeeded Zhou Zhang as minister of ceremonies.

sentences.zh: 愷性篤古，貴處士，每有征舉，必先巖穴。

translations.literal: Kai's nature valued antiquity and esteemed recluses; whenever there were summons and recommendations, he always began with mountain caves.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Kai revered classical precedent and held private scholars in high regard; whenever recommending men, he looked first to recluses in the hills.

sentences.zh: 論議引正，辭氣高雅。

translations.literal: In discussion he cited rectitude; his diction and tone were lofty and refined.
translations.idiomatic: His memorials were principled, and his phrasing was elevated and precise.

translations.literal: An asterisk marks editorial linkage to the dated clause that follows.
translations.idiomatic: The asterisk ties the next dated sentence to the preceding text.

sentences.zh: (永初)

translations.literal: (Yongchu)
translations.idiomatic: Yongchu era:

sentences.zh: *六年，代張敏為司空。

translations.literal: * In the sixth year, he replaced Zhang Min as Minister of Works.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongchu 6 (112 CE) he succeeded Zhang Min as minister of works.

sentences.zh: 元初二年，代夏勤為司徒。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yuanchu, he replaced Xia Qin as Minister of Education.
translations.idiomatic: In 115 CE he replaced Xia Qin as minister of education.

sentences.zh: 舊制，公卿、二千石、刺史不得行三年喪，由是內外觿職並廢喪禮。

translations.literal: By old regulation, dukes, ministers, officials of two thousand piculs, and inspectors-in-chief could not observe three years' mourning; hence inner and outer offices alike abandoned mourning rites.
translations.idiomatic: Statute barred three-year mourning for ministers, two-thousand-picul governors, and regional inspectors, so court and countryside alike let funeral observances lapse.

sentences.zh: 元初中，鄧太後詔長吏以下不為親行服者，不得典城選舉。

translations.literal: In the early Yuanchu period, Empress Dowager Deng issued an edict that subordinate magistrates and those below who did not wear mourning for parents could not administer a city or stand for selection.
translations.idiomatic: Early in the Yuanchu era, Empress Dowager Deng decreed that local officials who skipped mourning for parents could not govern a county or be nominated for office.

sentences.zh: 時有上言牧守宜同此制，詔下公卿，議者以為不便。

translations.literal: At the time someone memorialized that governors and shepherds ought likewise to be bound by this rule; an edict went down to the dukes and ministers, and those who debated thought it inconvenient.
translations.idiomatic: Someone urged the same rule for governors; the matter went to the high ministers, who judged it impractical.

sentences.zh: 愷獨議曰：「詔書所以為制服之科者，蓋崇化厲俗，以弘孝道也。

translations.literal: Kai alone argued, saying: "The purpose for which the edict lays down rules for mourning garments is to elevate transformation and brace custom, so as to magnify filial duty.
translations.idiomatic: Only Liu Kai replied that the mourning regulations existed to improve customs and exalt filial piety.

sentences.zh: 今刺史一州之表，二千石千里之師，[一]職在辯章百姓，宣美風俗，[二]尤宜尊重典禮，以身先之。

translations.literal: Now the inspector-in-chief is the model of a whole province, the official of two thousand piculs the teacher of a thousand li, [Note 1] their office being to sort and clarify the common people and proclaim beautiful customs, [Note 2] and they should all the more respect the ritual canon and take the lead with their own persons.
translations.idiomatic: Regional inspectors and two-thousand-picul governors are the moral leaders of their circuits; [Note 1] their job is to set the people in order and model good custom, [Note 2] so they above all must honor ritual and lead by example.

sentences.zh: 而議者不尋其端，至於牧守則雲不宜，是猶濁其源而望流清，曲其形而欲景直，不可得也。

translations.literal: Yet those who debate do not trace it to the beginning; when it comes to governors and shepherds they say it should not apply—this is like muddying the source yet expecting the stream to run clear, or bending the form yet wishing the shadow straight; it cannot be obtained.
translations.idiomatic: To exempt governors while demanding mourning from lesser officials is to foul the headwaters and expect clean water below, or to warp the body and demand a straight shadow—it cannot work.

sentences.zh: 」[三]太后從之。

translations.literal: " [Note 3] The Empress Dowager followed him.
translations.idiomatic: " [Note 3] The empress dowager accepted his view.

sentences.zh: 注[一]前書杜欽曰「即以二千石守千里之地，任兵馬之重，不宜去郡」也。

translations.literal: Note [1]: The former Han book: Du Qin said, "Since one holds two thousand piculs guarding a territory of a thousand li and bears the weight of arms and horses, one ought not leave the commandery."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Du Qin in the Han shu argued that two-thousand-picul governors, commanding troops across vast territories, should not leave their posts.

sentences.zh: 注[二]尚書曰：「九族既睦，辯章百姓。

translations.literal: Note [2]: The Documents says: "When the nine kindreds are harmonious, sort and clarify the common people."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The Shang shu reads, "When kin live in harmony, the hundred clans are governed with clarity."

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注云：「辯，別也。

translations.literal: " Zheng Xuan's commentary says: "Bian means distinguish."
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses "bian" as "to distinguish."

sentences.zh: 章，明也。」

translations.literal: Zhang means make bright."
translations.idiomatic: "Zhang" means to illuminate."

sentences.zh: 注[三]前書*[杜欽]*曰：「今淫僻之化流，而欲黎庶敦樸，猶濁其源而求流清也。」

translations.literal: Note [3]: The former Han book *[Du Qin]* said: "Now dissolute and aberrant transformation spreads, yet one wishes the black-haired common people to be honest and simple—it is like muddying the source yet seeking a clear stream."
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Du Qin warned that corrupting customs while expecting plain folk to stay honest was like fouling a spring and demanding pure water downstream.

sentences.zh: 時征西校尉任尚以奸利被征抵罪。

translations.literal: At the time Colonel West Ren Shang was summoned to answer for corruption and profit.
translations.idiomatic: Colonel of the West Ren Shang was recalled to face charges of graft.

sentences.zh: 尚曾副大將軍鄧騭，騭黨護之，而太尉馬英、司空李合承望騭旨，不復先請，即獨解尚臧錮，愷不肯與議。

translations.literal: Shang had once served as deputy to Grand General Deng Zhi; Zhi's faction shielded him, while Grand Commandant Ma Ying and Minister of Works Li He, complying with Zhi's intent, no longer sought prior approval but alone lifted Shang's confiscation and shackling; Kai refused to join in the deliberation.
translations.idiomatic: Ren Shang had served under Deng Zhi; Deng's clique protected him, and Grand Commandant Ma Ying and Minister of Works Li He, doing Deng's bidding, lifted Ren's penalties without prior clearance—Liu Kai refused to endorse their decision.

sentences.zh: 後尚書案其事，二府並受譴咎，[一]朝廷以此稱之。

translations.literal: Later the imperial secretariat investigated the affair, and both offices received blame, [Note 1] and the court for this praised him.
translations.idiomatic: When the secretariat reviewed the case, both ministers were censured; [Note 1] the court praised Liu Kai for holding the line.

sentences.zh: 注[一]二府即馬英、李合。

translations.literal: Note [1]: The two offices are Ma Ying and Li He.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The grand commandant Ma Ying and minister of works Li He.

sentences.zh: 注[二]易曰：「鼎折足，覆公餗。

translations.literal: Note [2]: The Changes says: "The cauldron breaks its foot, spilling the duke's gruel."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The Zhou yi speaks of a tripod whose leg snaps and spills the sacrifice—the classic image of failed ministers.

sentences.zh: 」鼎足，三公之象。

translations.literal: " The tripod's legs are the image of the Three Dukes.
translations.idiomatic: The three legs stand for the three highest ministers.

sentences.zh: 注[三]五品，五常之教也。

translations.literal: Note [3]: The five ranks are the teaching of the five constants.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: The "five ranks" refer to the five moral relationships.

sentences.zh: 三公燮理陰陽，敬敷五教也。

translations.literal: The Three Dukes harmonize yin and yang and reverently spread the five teachings.
translations.idiomatic: The three dukes balance heaven and earth and promulgate those teachings broadly.

sentences.zh: 注[四]尚書：「納舜於大麓，烈風雷雨不迷。

translations.literal: Note [4]: The Documents: "They brought Shun into the great forest; fierce wind, thunder, and rain did not make him lose his way."
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: The Shang shu says Shun was tested in the wild hills amid storms and never lost his bearings.

sentences.zh: 」史記曰「堯使舜入山林川澤，暴風雨，舜行不迷，堯以為聖」也。

translations.literal: " The Records says: "Yao sent Shun into mountains, forests, rivers, and marshes; storm and rain came; Shun walked without losing his way; Yao deemed him a sage."
translations.idiomatic: The Shiji adds that Yao sent Shun through hills and marshes in foul weather to prove his steadiness.

sentences.zh: 注[五]孔子曰：「不義而富*[且貴]*，於我如浮雲。

translations.literal: Note [5]: Confucius said: "Wealth and honor without righteousness are to me like floating clouds."
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: Confucius compared ill-gotten wealth and rank to clouds drifting past.

sentences.zh: 」孟子曰「我善養浩然之氣，而無怨害，則塞乎天地之閒」也。

translations.literal: " Mencius said: "I am good at nurturing flood-like qi, and without resentment or harm, then it fills between Heaven and Earth."
translations.idiomatic: Mencius claimed his flood-like moral qi could fill heaven and earth once freed from rancor.

sentences.zh: 言愷有仲尼、孟軻之德也。

translations.literal: This says Kai has the virtue of Zhongni and Meng Ke.
translations.idiomatic: The commentator ranks Liu Kai with Confucius and Mencius.

sentences.zh: 注[六]二司謂為司徒、司空。

translations.literal: Note [6]: The two departments mean Minister of Education and Minister of Works.
translations.idiomatic: Note [6]: The two ministers are education and works.

sentences.zh: 注[七]景慕以為法式。

translations.literal: Note [7]: Look up in admiration and take as model and law.
translations.idiomatic: Note [7]: To admire and imitate as a standard.

sentences.zh: 注[八]孔光，成帝時丞相，哀帝時免，後以日食征詣公車，復為丞相。

translations.literal: Note [8]: Kong Guang was chancellor under Emperor Cheng, dismissed under Emperor Ai; later, because of a solar eclipse, he was summoned to the public carriage office and again became chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Note [8]: Kong Guang served Emperor Cheng as chancellor, was dismissed under Ai, then was recalled after an eclipse omen and restored.

sentences.zh: 師丹，哀帝時代王莽為大司馬，後為大司空。

translations.literal: Shi Dan, under Emperor Ai, replaced Wang Mang as grand marshal of the army, later becoming grand minister of works.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Dan succeeded Wang Mang as grand marshal under Emperor Ai and later became minister of works.

sentences.zh: 鄧彪，明帝時為太尉，章帝元和元年賜策罷，和帝即位，以彪為太傅，錄尚書事。

translations.literal: Deng Biao was grand commandant under Emperor Ming; in the first year of Yuanhe of Emperor Zhang he was dismissed by edict with bestowed tallies; when Emperor He ascended, Biao was made grand tutor and recorded affairs of the masters of writing.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Biao was grand commandant under Emperor Ming, retired with honors in 84 CE, and returned as grand tutor and chief of the secretariat under Emperor He.

sentences.zh: 張酺，和帝永元五年為太尉，後策免，十六年復為司徒。

translations.literal: Zhang Pu was grand commandant in the fifth year of Yongyuan of Emperor He, later dismissed by edict, and in the sixteenth year again became Minister of Education.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Pu served Emperor He as grand commandant in 93 CE, was removed, then returned as minister of education in 104 CE.

sentences.zh: 注[九]二代謂父子俱禁錮。

translations.literal: Note [9]: Two generations means father and son both subjected to proscription.
translations.idiomatic: Note [9]: "Two generations" means both father and son were barred from office.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]比，類也。

translations.literal: Note [10]: Bi means category.
translations.idiomatic: Note [10]: "Bi" means analogy or parallel case.

sentences.zh: 以邠類叔孫光，亦錮及子也。

translations.literal: By analogy with Bin as parallel to Shusun Guang, likewise proscription extended to the son.
translations.idiomatic: The parallel drawn between Bin and Shusun Guang shows how the ban could reach the next generation too.

sentences.zh: 比音庇。

translations.literal: Bi is read like the homophone meaning shelter.
translations.idiomatic: The character bi here rhymes with the word for "shelter."

sentences.zh: 注[一一]公羊傳曰：「曹公孫會自鄸出奔宋，畔也。

translations.literal: Note [11]: The Gongyang Commentary says: "Gongsun Hui of Cao fled from Weng to Song—it was rebellion."
translations.idiomatic: Note [11]: The Gongyang zhuan quotes: Gongsun Hui of Cao fled Weng for Song in revolt.

sentences.zh: 曷為不言畔？

translations.literal: Why is it not said "rebel"?
translations.idiomatic: Why does the text avoid calling it rebellion?

sentences.zh: 為公子喜時之後諱也，春秋為賢者諱也。

translations.literal: It is to conceal for the sake of the descendants of Lord Xishi; the Spring and Autumn conceals for worthies.
translations.idiomatic: The classic spares the heirs of Prince Xishi; the Chunqiu routinely glosses over faults of the virtuous.

sentences.zh: 何賢乎公子喜時？

translations.literal: In what way was Lord Xishi worthy?
translations.idiomatic: What made Prince Xishi admirable?

sentences.zh: 讓國也。

translations.literal: He yielded the state.
translations.idiomatic: He had surrendered his claim to the state.

sentences.zh: 君子之善善也長，惡惡也短。

translations.literal: For the gentleman, praising good is long; condemning evil is short.
translations.idiomatic: The gentleman dwells on merit at length but passes quickly over fault.

sentences.zh: 惡惡止其身，善善及子孫。

translations.literal: Condemning evil stops at his person; praising good reaches descendants.
translations.idiomatic: Blame attaches only to the man himself; praise may bless later generations.

sentences.zh: 賢者子孫，故君子為其諱也。」

translations.literal: They are the descendants of a worthy, hence the gentleman conceals for him."
translations.idiomatic: Because they descend from a worthy man, the classic softens the record for their sake."

sentences.zh: 注[一二]今尚書呂刑篇曰：「上刑適輕下服，下刑適重上服。

translations.literal: Note [12]: The present Documents, "Lu Punishments" chapter, says: "When upper punishments fit the lighter crime, the lower ranks submit; when lower punishments fit the graver crime, the upper ranks submit."
translations.idiomatic: Note [12]: Today's Shang shu "Lu xing" reads that lighter guilt may still draw a senior penalty, and graver guilt a mitigated one, depending on circumstance.

sentences.zh: 」謂二罪俱發，原其本情，須有虧減，故言適輕適重。

translations.literal: " This means when two crimes arise together, tracing their root disposition requires reduction or increase, hence it speaks of fitting light or fitting heavy.
translations.idiomatic: When two offenses coincide, judgment weighs intent and may lighten or stiffen the sentence accordingly.

sentences.zh: 此言「挾輕挾重」，意亦不殊，但與今尚書不同耳。

translations.literal: This statement "bearing light and bearing heavy" differs in wording but not in meaning—only it is not the same as today's Documents.
translations.idiomatic: The phrase about weighing lighter and graver penalties matches the idea though the wording differs from the current Shang shu text.

sentences.zh: 注[一三]左傳曰：「刑濫則懼及善人。」

translations.literal: Note [13]: The Zuo Commentary says: "When punishments run to excess, fear reaches good men."
translations.idiomatic: Note [13]: The Zuozhuan warns that excessive punishment terrifies the innocent.

sentences.zh: 注[一四]尚書周穆王曰：「有邦有土，告汝詳刑。

translations.literal: Note [14]: The Documents, King Mu of Zhou, says: "You who hold states and land, I instruct you in careful punishments."
translations.idiomatic: Note [14]: King Mu in the Documents tells rulers with territory to judge cases with care.

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注云：「詳，審察之也。」

translations.literal: " Zheng Xuan's commentary says: "Xiang means examine closely."
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses xiang as thorough review.

sentences.zh: 視事三年，以疾乞骸骨，久乃許之，下河南尹禮秩如前。

translations.literal: After three years in office he requested retirement on grounds of illness; only after a long time was it granted; orders went to the Governor of Henan that ritual rank remain as before.
translations.idiomatic: After three years he asked to retire for illness; the court delayed, then let him go and told the Henan governor to keep his stipend and honors.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，卒於家。

translations.literal: After more than a year, he died at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home a little over a year later.

sentences.zh: 詔使者護喪事，賜東園秘器，錢五十萬，布千匹。

translations.literal: An edict ordered envoys to supervise the funeral; there were granted Eastern Park burial gear, five hundred thousand cash, and one thousand bolts of cloth.
translations.idiomatic: The court sent officers to oversee the rites, granted a lacquered coffin from the imperial workshop, five hundred thousand cash, and a thousand bolts of cloth.

sentences.zh: 少子茂，字叔盛，亦好禮讓，歷位出納，[一]桓帝時為司空。

translations.literal: The younger son Mao, courtesy name Shusheng, likewise loved ritual deference; he successively held outgoing-and-incoming posts, [Note 1] and under Emperor Huan became Minister of Works.
translations.idiomatic: His younger son Liu Mao (Shusheng) also prized yielding; he rose through the secretariat [Note 1] and became minister of works under Emperor Huan.

sentences.zh: 會司隸校尉李膺等抵罪，而南陽太守成□、太原太守劉□下獄當死，茂與太尉陳蕃、司徒劉矩共上書訟之。

translations.literal: When Metropolitan Commandant Li Ying and others were charged, and the governors of Nanyang Cheng (character missing) and Taiyuan Liu (character missing) were cast into prison facing death, Mao with Grand Commandant Chen Fan and Minister of Education Liu Ju jointly submitted memorials pleading for them.
translations.idiomatic: When Li Ying and others fell under indictment and the governors of Nanyang (surname Cheng) and Taiyuan (surname Liu) were jailed under sentence of death, Liu Mao joined Chen Fan and Liu Ju in pleading for them.

sentences.zh: 帝不悅，有司承旨劾奏三公，茂遂坐免。

translations.literal: The Emperor was displeased; the offices, complying with intent, impeached the Three Dukes; Mao was therefore removed by association.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was angry; compliant officials impeached the three highest ministers, and Liu Mao lost his post.

sentences.zh: 建寧中，復為太中大夫，卒於官。

translations.literal: During Jianning he again became grand counselor of the palace and died in office.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Ling he returned as grand counselor and died in harness.

sentences.zh: 注[一]出納謂尚書，喉舌之官也。

translations.literal: Note [1]: Outgoing-and-incoming means the masters of writing—the throat-and-tongue office.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The secretariat speaks for the throne to the realm and back; it is called the "throat and tongue."

sentences.zh: 出謂受上言宣於下，納謂聽下言傳於上。

translations.literal: Outgoing means receiving the ruler's words and proclaiming them below; incoming means hearing words from below and transmitting them above.
translations.idiomatic: Outgoing edicts carry the court's voice downward; incoming memorials carry local voices up.

sentences.zh: 周盤字堅伯，汝南安成人，征士燮之宗也。

translations.literal: Zhou Pan, courtesy name Jianbo, was a native of Ancheng in Runan, of the same clan as the summoned scholar Xie.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Pan (Jianbo) came from Ancheng in Runan and belonged to the lineage of the recluse scholar Xie.

sentences.zh: [一]祖父業，建武初為天水太守。

translations.literal: [Note 1] His grandfather Ye was governor of Tianshui at the beginning of Jianwu.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] His grandfather Zhou Ye served as governor of Tianshui early in the Guangwu reign.

sentences.zh: 盤少游京師，學古文尚書、洪範五行、左氏傳，好禮有行，非典謨不言，諸儒宗之。

translations.literal: Pan in youth traveled to the capital and studied the Old Text Documents, the Hongfan Five Phases, and Zuo's Commentary; he loved ritual and had conduct; he did not speak except on canonical models and plans; the scholars honored him as their teacher.
translations.idiomatic: As a young man Zhou Pan studied in Luoyang—the Old Text Shang shu, Hongfan astrology, and the Zuozhuan—kept ritual discipline, spoke only on canonical matters, and became a scholarly authority.

sentences.zh: 居貧養母，儉薄不充。

translations.literal: Dwelling in poverty he nourished his mother; austerity was thin and insufficient.
translations.idiomatic: He kept his mother on very little and rarely had enough.

sentences.zh: 嘗誦詩至汝墳之卒章，慨然而歎，[二]乃解韋帶，就孝廉之舉。

translations.literal: Once when reciting the Poetry he reached the final stanza of "The Banks of Ru," he sighed with emotion, [Note 2] then undid his leather belt and accepted recommendation as filially pious and incorrupt.
translations.idiomatic: Reciting "The Banks of Ru" he broke off at the closing stanza with a deep sigh, [Note 2] shed his rustic belt, and asked to be nominated as filial and incorrupt.

sentences.zh: [三]和帝初，拜謁者，除任城長，遷陽夏、重合令，[四]頻歷三城，皆有惠政。

translations.literal: [Note 3] At the beginning of Emperor He's reign he was appointed herald, assigned magistrate of Rencheng, promoted magistrate of Yangxia and Chonghe, [Note 4] successively passing through three cities, all with benevolent government.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 3] Under Emperor He he became herald, then magistrate of Rencheng, Yangxia, and Chonghe in turn, [Note 4] earning a reputation for kindness at each post.

sentences.zh: 後思母，□官還鄉里。

translations.literal: Later, longing for his mother, he left office and returned to his home village.
translations.idiomatic: Missing his mother, he left office and went home.

sentences.zh: 及母歿，哀至幾於毀滅，服終，遂廬於頤側。

translations.literal: When his mother died, grief reached nearly to self-destruction; when mourning ended, he built a hut beside her tomb mound.
translations.idiomatic: Her death nearly broke him with grief; after the mourning period he lived in a mourning hut beside her grave.

sentences.zh: 教授門徒常千人。

translations.literal: He instructed disciples, often a thousand.
translations.idiomatic: His lecture hall regularly drew a thousand students.

sentences.zh: 注[一]燮自有傳。

translations.literal: Note [1]: Xie has his own biography.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Xie has a separate biography in this history.

sentences.zh: 注[二]韓詩曰：「汝墳，辭家也。

translations.literal: Note [2]: The Han Poetry says: "The Banks of Ru—leaving home."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The Han version of the Songs glosses "The Banks of Ru" as a poem about leaving home.

sentences.zh: 」其卒章曰：「魴魚赬尾，王室如□，雖則如娓，父母孔邇。

translations.literal: " Its final stanza says: "The bream's tail is red; the royal house is like a missing graph for flame; though it seems gentle, father and mother are very near."
translations.idiomatic: The closing lines liken a weary court to a blazing furnace (the text has a lacuna) yet stress staying near one's parents.

sentences.zh: 」薛君章句：「赬，赤也。

translations.literal: " Xue Jun's chapter-and-verse gloss: "Cheng means red."
translations.idiomatic: Xue's commentary glosses cheng as crimson.

sentences.zh: □，烈火也。

translations.literal: The missing character names blazing fire.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss supplies a character meaning fierce flame.

sentences.zh: 孔，甚也。

translations.literal: Kong means very.
translations.idiomatic: "Kong" intensifies the adjective as "greatly."

sentences.zh: 邇，近也。

translations.literal: Er means near.
translations.idiomatic: "Er" means "close at hand."

sentences.zh: 言魴魚勞則尾赤，君子勞苦則顏色變。

translations.literal: It means when the bream is weary its tail turns red; when the gentleman is worn his complexion changes.
translations.idiomatic: Just as overwork reddens a fish's tail, hardship changes a gentleman's face.

sentences.zh: 以王室政教如烈火矣，猶觸冒而仕者，以父母甚迫近饑寒之憂，為此祿仕。」

translations.literal: Because royal government and instruction are like fierce flame, yet those who still brave it and take office do so because father and mother press very near with hunger and cold—hence they take this salary office."
translations.idiomatic: Even though court rule burns hot as fire, men still serve because parents face starvation at their elbow—they take salary posts from sheer necessity."

sentences.zh: 注[三]以韋皮為帶，未仕之服也。

translations.literal: Note [3]: Using hide for a belt is the dress of one not yet in office.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: A leather belt marks a scholar who has not yet taken appointment.

sentences.zh: 求仕則服革帶，故解之。

translations.literal: Seeking office then means wearing a leather belt; hence he undid it.
translations.idiomatic: Office seekers wear stiff belts, so he removed his rustic strap to signal readiness.

sentences.zh: 賈山上書曰「布衣韋帶之士」也。

translations.literal: Jia Shan's memorial says "gentlemen in cloth robes and hide belts."
translations.idiomatic: Jia Shan called such men "plain cloth and leather belt" scholars.

sentences.zh: 注[四]陽夏屬淮南郡。

translations.literal: Note [4]: Yangxia belonged to Huainan commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: Yangxia county lay in Huainan commandery.

sentences.zh: 重合屬勃海郡。

translations.literal: Chonghe belonged to Bohai commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Chonghe county was in Bohai commandery.

sentences.zh: 公府三辟，皆以有道特徵，盤語友人曰：「昔方回、支父嗇神養和，不以榮利滑其生術。

translations.literal: The offices of the Three Dukes thrice summoned him, all with special summons for possessing the Way; Pan told a friend: "Of old Fang Hui and Zhi Fu treasured spirit and nurtured harmony; they did not let glory and profit disturb their art of life.
translations.idiomatic: The thrice-summoned "man of the Way" told friends how Fang Hui and Zhi Fu guarded body and breath and never let ambition spoil their longevity arts.

sentences.zh: [一]吾親以沒矣，從物何為？

translations.literal: [Note 1] My parents are already gone—what point is there in pursuing things?
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] My parents are dead; why chase career now?

sentences.zh: 」遂不應。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he did not respond.
translations.idiomatic: He refused every summons.

sentences.zh: [二]建光元年，年七十三，歲朝會集諸生，講論終日，[三]因令其二子曰：「吾日者夢見先師東裡先生，與我講於陰堂之奧。

translations.literal: [Note 2] In the first year of Jianguang, age seventy-three, at the New Year gathering he assembled the students and lectured all day, [Note 3] then told his two sons: "The other day I dreamed I saw my former teacher Master Dongli discoursing with me in the interior recess of the shaded hall.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 2] At seventy-three, on New Year's day of 121 CE, he lectured students from dawn to dusk, [Note 3] then told his sons he had dreamed his teacher Dongli debating with him in the inner chamber.

sentences.zh: 」[四]既而長歎：「豈吾齒之盡乎！

translations.literal: " [Note 4] Then he sighed long: "Could it be my teeth are finished!
translations.idiomatic: " [Note 4] Then he sighed, "Has my span truly run out?

sentences.zh: 若命終之日，桐棺足以週身，外幟足以周棺，斂形懸封，濯衣幅巾。

translations.literal: If it be the day fate ends, a paulownia coffin is enough to surround the body; outer pall is enough to wrap the coffin; gather the form with suspended sealing; washed clothes and wrapped headcloth.
translations.idiomatic: When I die, let a plain paulownia coffin cover me, a simple pall wrap it, lower me straight down without a ramp, dress me in washed linens and a cloth headwrap.

sentences.zh: [五]編二尺四寸簡，寫堯典一篇，並刀筆各一，以置棺前，雲不忘聖道。

translations.literal: [Note 5] Compile slips two chi four cun long, write out one chapter of the Canon of Yao, together with one knife and one brush each, to place before the coffin, saying one does not forget the sage Way.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 5] Burial slips twenty-four cun long should carry the "Canon of Yao," plus knife and brush, laid before the coffin as a pledge to the classical tradition.

sentences.zh: 」其月望日，無病忽終，學者以為知命焉。

translations.literal: " On the fifteenth of that month, without illness he suddenly expired; scholars deemed him one who knew fate.
translations.idiomatic: On the full moon that month he died suddenly, apparently healthy—followers called it knowing one's allotted span.

sentences.zh: 注[一]嗇，愛惜也。

translations.literal: Note [1]: Se means cherish and spare.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: "Se" means to husband carefully.

sentences.zh: 滑，亂也。

translations.literal: Hua means disturb.
translations.idiomatic: "Hua" means to throw into confusion.

sentences.zh: 列仙傳曰：「方回，堯時隱人也。

translations.literal: The Arrayed Immortals says: "Fang Hui was a recluse in Yao's time.
translations.idiomatic: The Liexian zhuan calls Fang Hui a recluse under Emperor Yao.

sentences.zh: 堯聘之，練食雲母，隱於五柞山。

translations.literal: Yao summoned him; he ate refined cloud-mica and hid on Mount Wuzuo.
translations.idiomatic: Yao courted him; he lived on mica on Mount Wuzuo.

sentences.zh: 至夏啟末，為人所劫，閉之室中，從求道，回化而去。」

translations.literal: At the end of Xia Qi he was seized by men, shut in a chamber; they sought the Way from him; Hui transformed and departed."
translations.idiomatic: Late in Qi of Xia robbers locked him up demanding immortality lore; he vanished in transformation."

sentences.zh: 高士傳曰：「堯舜各以天下讓支父，支父曰：『予適有勞憂之病，方且療之，未暇理天下也。

translations.literal: The Biographies of Lofty Gentlemen says: "Yao and Shun each yielded the realm to Zhi Fu; Zhi Fu said: 'I happen to have a weary illness and am just treating it; I have no leisure to order the realm.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Gaoshi zhuan records Zhi Fu refusing both Yao and Shang while he nursed an illness.

sentences.zh: 』」莊子作「支伯」。

translations.literal: The Zhuangzi writes the name as "Zhi Bo."
translations.idiomatic: The Zhuangzi spells the recluse's name "Zhi Bo."

sentences.zh: 注[二]物猶事也。

translations.literal: Note [2]: Wu means affair.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: "Wu" here means worldly pursuits.

sentences.zh: 注[三]歲朝，歲旦。

translations.literal: Note [3]: New Year's court gathering means New Year's dawn.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: "Sui chao" is New Year's morning assembly.

sentences.zh: 注[四]東南隅謂之奧，陰堂幽暗之室。

translations.literal: Note [4]: The southeast corner is called the recess; the shaded hall is a dim chamber.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: The inner southeast corner is the "recess" of the hall—the darkest part.

sentences.zh: 又入其奧，死之象也。

translations.literal: Moreover entering its recess is an image of death.
translations.idiomatic: Dreaming one has entered that recess foreshadows death.

sentences.zh: 注[五]斂形謂衣覆其形。

translations.literal: Note [5]: Gathering the form means clothing covers the form.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: "Gathering the form" means dressing the corpse.

sentences.zh: 懸封謂直下棺，不為埏道也。

translations.literal: Suspended sealing means lowering the coffin straight down; not making a ramp passage.
translations.idiomatic: Direct burial without a sloping entrance ramp.

sentences.zh: 濯衣，浣衣也，不更新制。

translations.literal: Washed clothes means rinsed clothes; not newly tailored.
translations.idiomatic: Ordinary washed garments, not newly sewn grave clothes.

sentences.zh: 幅巾，不加冠也。

translations.literal: Wrapped headcloth means not adding a cap.
translations.idiomatic: A simple cloth headwrap instead of a formal cap.

sentences.zh: 封音窆。

translations.literal: Feng is pronounced bian.
translations.idiomatic: "Feng" (burial) is read like "bian."

sentences.zh: 盤同郡蔡順，字君仲，亦以至孝稱。

translations.literal: From the same commandery as Pan was Cai Shun, courtesy name Junzhong, likewise famed for utmost filial piety.
translations.idiomatic: Cai Shun (Junzhong) of the same county was celebrated for filial devotion.

sentences.zh: [一]順少孤，養母。

translations.literal: [Note 1] Shun was orphaned young and nourished his mother.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] Orphaned early, Cai Shun raised his mother alone.

sentences.zh: 嘗出求薪，有客卒至，[二]母望順不還，乃噬其指，[三]順即心動，□薪馳歸，跪問其故。

translations.literal: Once when he went out seeking firewood a guest suddenly arrived; [Note 2] his mother, watching for Shun's return, bit her finger; [Note 3] Shun's heart stirred at once; he left his firewood bundle and galloped home, knelt and asked the reason.
translations.idiomatic: Once while gathering firewood an unexpected guest arrived; [Note 2] his anxious mother bit her finger; [Note 3] Cai Shun felt it, dropped his bundle, and raced home to kneel and ask why.

sentences.zh: 母曰：「有急客來，吾噬指以悟汝耳。

translations.literal: His mother said: "An urgent guest came; I bit my finger to awaken you.
translations.idiomatic: She explained that she had nipped her finger to signal him.

sentences.zh: 」母年九十，以壽終。

translations.literal: " His mother at age ninety ended her span.
translations.idiomatic: His mother died peacefully at ninety.

sentences.zh: 未及得葬，裡中災，火將逼其捨，順抱伏棺柩，號哭叫天，火遂越燒它室，順獨得免。

translations.literal: Before burial could be completed, disaster struck the hamlet; fire was about to approach his house; Shun embraced and lay upon the coffin, wailing and crying to Heaven; the fire then leapt past to burn other chambers; Shun alone was spared.
translations.idiomatic: Before the funeral a neighborhood fire threatened his home; he threw himself on the coffin and wept to heaven—the flames skipped his hut.

sentences.zh: 太守韓崇召為東合祭酒。

translations.literal: Governor Han Chong summoned him as libationer of the eastern pavilion.
translations.idiomatic: Governor Han Chong named him libationer for the eastern bureau.

sentences.zh: 母平生畏雷，自亡後，每有雷震，順輒圜頤泣，曰：「順在此。

translations.literal: His mother all her life feared thunder; after her death, whenever thunder rolled Shun would pace round the tomb mound weeping, saying: "Shun is here.
translations.idiomatic: She had feared thunder; after her death he paced her burial mound at every storm, calling out that her son was beside her.

sentences.zh: 」崇聞之，每雷輒為差車馬到墓所。

translations.literal: " Chong heard of it and every thunder dispatched carriage and horses to the tomb site.
translations.idiomatic: When Han Chong heard this, he sent a carriage to the grave whenever it thundered.

sentences.zh: 後太守鮑觿舉孝廉，順不能遠離墳墓，遂不就。

translations.literal: Later Governor Bao Xi recommended him as filially pious and incorrupt; Shun could not go far from the tomb mound and therefore did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: Later Governor Bao Xi nominated him, but he would not leave his mother's grave and declined.

sentences.zh: 年八十，終於家。

translations.literal: At age eighty he ended at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home at eighty.

sentences.zh: 注[一]汝南先賢傳曰：「蔡順事母至孝。

translations.literal: Note [1]: The Record of Worthies Past of Runan says: "Cai Shun served his mother with utmost filial piety.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The Runan gazetteer praises Cai Shun's devotion.

sentences.zh: 井桔□朽，在母生年上，而順憂，不敢理之。

translations.literal: The well windlass was rotten (character missing) on the branch of his mother's birth year, and Shun worried, not daring to repair it.
translations.idiomatic: The well sweep over his mother's birth-year pillar rotted; he feared touching it.

sentences.zh: 俄而有扶老籐生，繞之，遂堅固焉。」

translations.literal: Soon there grew an old-man-support vine winding it, and it became firm."
translations.idiomatic: Overnight a vine twined the pole and strengthened it."

sentences.zh: 注[二]卒音千訥反。

translations.literal: Note [2]: Zu is read qian-ne fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The word zu here rhymes like "cen" in the entering tone.

sentences.zh: 注[三]噬，嚙也。

translations.literal: Note [3]: Shi means bite.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: "Shi" means to bite down.

sentences.zh: 趙咨字文楚，東郡燕人也。

translations.literal: Zhao Zi, courtesy name Wenchu, was a man of Yan in Dong commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Zi (Wenchu) came from Yan in Dong commandery.

sentences.zh: [一]父暢，為博士。

translations.literal: [Note 1] His father Chang was an erudite.
translations.idiomatic: [Note 1] His father Zhao Chang held an erudite chair.

sentences.zh: 咨少孤，有孝行，州郡召舉孝廉，並不就。

translations.literal: Zi was orphaned young and had filial conduct; the province and commandery summoned and recommended him as filially pious and incorrupt; he did not go to any.
translations.idiomatic: Orphaned young and famously dutiful, he turned down every nomination as filial and incorrupt.

sentences.zh: 注[一]燕故城，今滑州胙城縣也，古南燕之國也。

translations.literal: Note [1]: Yan's old walled city is today's Zuocheng county in Huazhou—it was the ancient state of Southern Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Ancient Yan stood where modern Zuocheng in Huazhou lies—the old Southern Yan.

sentences.zh: 盜嘗夜往劫之，咨恐母驚懼，乃先至門迎盜，因請為設食，謝曰：「老母八十，疾病須養，居貧，朝夕無儲，乞少置衣糧。

translations.literal: Robbers once went by night to rob him; Zi feared his mother would be frightened; he therefore first reached the gate to welcome the robbers and requested to set out food for them, apologizing: "My old mother is eighty; illness requires nurturing; dwelling in poverty, morning and evening there are no stores; I beg you leave a little clothing and grain.
translations.idiomatic: When bandits broke in at night he met them at the gate before his mother woke; he offered food and asked only that they leave enough for his sick mother.

sentences.zh: 」妻子物余，一無所請。

translations.literal: " As for wife and children's goods beyond, he requested nothing at all.
translations.idiomatic: He asked them to spare food and clothing only—nothing else for wife or children.

sentences.zh: 盜皆籩歎，跪而辭曰：「所犯無狀，干暴賢者。

translations.literal: The robbers all sighed; kneeling they declined saying: "What we committed lacks proper form; we have offended and violated a worthy.
translations.idiomatic: Ashamed, they knelt and withdrew, apologizing for offending a worthy man.

sentences.zh: 」言畢奔出，咨追以物與之，不及。

translations.literal: " When the words were finished they ran out; Zi pursued to give them goods but did not catch up.
translations.idiomatic: They fled; he chased them with gifts but could not overtake them.

sentences.zh: 由此益知名。

translations.literal: From this he became still more known by name.
translations.idiomatic: His reputation spread further.

sentences.zh: 征拜議郎，辭疾不到，詔書切讓，州郡以禮發遣，前後再三，不得已應召。

translations.literal: He was summoned and appointed gentleman consultant; he declined illness and did not arrive; edicts sternly rebuked; province and commandery sent him off with ritual—before and after repeatedly thrice; unavoidably he answered the summons.
translations.idiomatic: Summoned as gentleman consultant, he pleaded illness until imperial rebukes and repeated escorts forced him to Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 復拜東海相。

translations.literal: He was again appointed chancellor of Donghai.
translations.idiomatic: He was named chancellor of Donghai.

sentences.zh: 之官，道經滎陽，令敦煌曹暠，咨之故孝廉也，[一]迎路謁候，咨不為留。

translations.literal: Going to office, his route passed Xingyang; Magistrate Cao Gao, a man of Dunhuang whom Zi had once recommended as filially pious and incorrupt— [Note 1] greeted him on the road with homage; Zi did not stop for him.
translations.idiomatic: En route to Donghai he passed Xingyang, where Magistrate Cao Gao—once Zhao Zi's nominee as filial and incorrupt—lined the road; Zhao Zi would not stop.

sentences.zh: 暠送至亭次，望塵不及，謂主簿曰：「趙君名重，今過界不見，必為天下笑！

translations.literal: Gao escorted him to the pavilion station, gazed at the dust and did not catch up; he said to the chief clerk: "Lord Zhao's name is weighty; now passing the boundary without seeing him—surely we will be laughed at by the realm!
translations.idiomatic: Cao chased him to the post station but only saw dust; he told his clerk that missing such an eminent visitor would make them a laughingstock.

sentences.zh: 」即□印綬，追至東海。
謁咨畢，辭歸家。
其為時人所貴若此。
注[一]咨為敦煌太守時，薦暠為孝廉。
咨在官清簡，計日受奉，豪黨畏其儉節。
視事三年，以疾自乞，征拜議郎。
抗疾京師，將終，告其故吏朱只、蕭建等，使薄斂素棺，籍以黃壤，[一]欲令速朽，早歸后土，不聽子孫改之。
乃遺書□子胤曰：「夫含氣之倫，有生必終，蓋天地之常期，自然之至數。
是以通人達士，鑒茲性命，以存亡為晦明，死生為朝夕，故其生也不為娛，亡也不知戚。
夫亡者，元氣去體，貞魂遊散，反素復始，歸於無端。
[二]既已消僕，還合糞土。
土為□物，豈有性情，而欲制其厚薄，調其燥濕邪？
但以生者之情，不忍見形之毀，乃有掩骼埋窆之制。
易曰：『古之葬者，衣以薪，藏之中野，後世聖人易之以棺幟。
』[三]棺幟之造，自黃帝始。
[四]爰自陶唐，逮於虞、夏，猶尚簡樸，或瓦或木，及至殷人而有加焉。
[五] 周室因之，制兼二代。
復重以牆翣之飾，[六]表以旌銘之儀，[七]招復含斂之禮，[八]殯葬宅兆之期，[九]棺幟周重之制，[一０]衣衾稱襲之數，[一一]其事煩而害實，品物碎而難備。
然而秩爵異級，貴賤殊等。
自成、康以下，其典稍乖。
至於戰國，漸至頹陵，[一二]法度衰毀，上下僭雜。
終使晉侯請隧，[一三]秦伯殉葬，[一四]陳大夫設參門之木，宋司馬造石幟之奢。
[一五]爰暨暴秦，違道廢德，滅三代之制，興淫邪之法，國貲糜於三泉，人力單于酈墓，玩好窮於糞土，伎巧費於窀穸。
[一六]自生民以來，厚終之敝，未有若此者。
雖有仲尼重明周禮，[一七]墨子勉以古道，猶不能御也。
[一八]是以華夏之士，爭相陵尚，違禮之本，事禮之末，務禮之華，□禮之實，單家竭財，以相營赴。
廢事生而營終亡，替所養而為厚葬，[一九]豈雲聖人制禮之意乎？
記曰：『喪雖有禮，哀為主矣。
』又曰：『喪與其易也寧戚。
』今則不然，並棺合幟，以為孝愷，豐貲重襚，以昭惻隱，[二０]吾所不取也。
昔舜葬蒼梧，二妃不從。
[二一]豈有匹配之會，守常之所乎？
